gibstaff: A staff to gauge water or to push a boat; formerly, a staff used in fighting beasts on the stage.
giddily: With the head seeming to turn or reel.
Inconstantly; unsteadily; with various turnings; as, to roam about giddily .
giddiness: The state of being giddy or vertiginous; vertigo; a sensation of reeling or whirling, when the body loses the power of preserving its balance or a steady attitude, or when objects at rest appear to reel, tremble or whirl; a swimming of the head.
giddy: Vertiginous; reeling; whirling; having in the head a sensation of a circular motion or swimming; or having lost the power of preserving the balance of the body, and therefore wavering and inclined to fall, as in the case of some diseases and of drunkenness. In walking on timber aloft, or looking down a precipice, we are apt to be giddy.
That renders giddy; that induces giddiness; as a giddy highth; a giddy precipice.
Rotary; whirling; running round with celerity. The giddy motion of the whirling mill.
Inconstant; unstable; changeable. You are as giddy and volatile as ever.
giddy-head: A person without thought or judgment.
gier-eagle: A fowl of the eagle kind, mentioned in Leviticus 11:18.
gieseckite: A mineral of a rhomboidal form and compact texture, of a gray or brown color, and nearly as hard as calcarious spar.
gif: The old but true spelling of if.
gift: A present; any thing given or bestowed; any thing, the property of which is voluntarily transferred by one person to another without compensation; a donation. It is applicable to any thing movable or immovable.
The act of giving or conferring.
The right or power of giving or bestowing. The prince has the gift of many lucrative offices.
An offering or oblation. If thou bring thy gift to the altar. Watthew 5:23.
Areward. Let thy gifts be to thyself. Daniel 5:17.
gifted: or a. Endowed by nature with any power or faculty; furnished with any particular talent.
gifting: Endowing with any power or faculty.
gig: To fish with a gig or fishgig.
Any little thing that is whirled round in play.
A light carriage with one pair of wheels, drawn by one horse; a chair or chaise.
gigantic: Of extraordinary size; very large; huge; like a giant. A man of gigantic stature.
Enormous; very great or mighty; as gigantic deeds; gigantic wickedness. Gigantical and gigantine, for gigantic, rarely or never used.
gigantology: An account or description of giants.
giggle: To laugh with short catches of the breath or voice; to laugh in a silly, puerile manner; to titter; to grin with childish levity or mirth.
A kind of laugh, with short catches of the voice or breath.
gilbertine: One of a religious order, so named from Gilbert, lord of Sempringham, in Lincolnshire, England.
Belonging to the monastic order, mentioned above.
gild: pret. and pp. gilded or gilt.

To overlay with gold, either in leaf or powder, or in amalgam with quicksilver; to overspread with a thin covering of gold; as the gilt frame of a mirror. Her joy in gilded chariots when alive, And love of ombre after death survive.
To cover with any yellow matter.
To adorn with luster; to render bright. No more the rising sun shall gild the morn.
To illuminate; to brighten. Let oft good humor, mild and gay, Gild the calm evening of your day.
gilded: Overlaid with gold leaf or liquid; illuminated.
gilder: One who gilds; one whose occupation is to overlay things with gold.
A Dutch coin of the value of 20 stivers, about 38 cents, or one shilling and ninepence sterling. It is usually written guilder.
gilding: Overlaying with gold; giving a fair external appearance.
The art or practice of overlaying things with gold leaf or liquid.
That which is laid on in overlaying with gold.
gill: The organ of respiration in fishes, consisting of a cartilaginous or bony arch, attached to the bones of the head, and furnished on the exterior convex side with a multitude of fleshy leaves, or fringed vascular fibrils, resembling plumes, and of a red color in a healthy state. The water is admitted by the gill-opening, and acts upon the blood as it circulates in the fibrils. Other animals also breathe by gills, as frogs in their tadpole state, lobsters. Fishes perform respiration under water by the gills.
The flap that hangs below the beak of a fowl.
In England, a pair of wheels and a frame on which timber is conveyed.
A measure of capacity, containing the fourth part of a pint. It is said to be in some places in England, half a pint.
gill-flap: A membrane attached to the posterior edge of the gill-lid, immediately closing the gill-opening.
gill-opening: The aperture of a fish or other animal, by which water is admitted to the gills.
gillhouse: A place where gill is sold.
gillyflower: The name of certain plants. The clove gilly-flower is of the genus Dianthus, or carnation pink; the stock gillyflower is the Cheiranthus; the queen’s gillyflower is the Hesperis.
gilt: of gild. Overlaid with gold leaf, or washed with gold; illuminated; adorned.
Gold laid on the surface of a thing; gilding.
In England, a young female pig.
gilthead: In ichthyology, a fish or a genus of fishes, the Sparus, of many species; so named from their color, or from a golden spot between the eyes.
gilttail: A worm so called from its yellow tail.
gimbal: A brass ring by which a sea compass is suspended in its box, by means of which the card is kept in a horizontal position, notwithstanding the rolling of the ship.
gimblet: A borer; small instrument with a pointed screw at the end, for boring holes in wood by turning. It is applied only to small instruments; a large instrument of the like kind is called an auger.
In seamen’s language, to turn round an anchor by the stock; a motion resembling that of the turning of a gimblet.
gimcrack: A trivial mechanism; a device; a toy; a pretty thing.
gimp: A kind of silk twist or edging.
gin: A contraction of Geneva, a distilled spirit.

A machine or instrument by which the mechanical powers are employed in aid of human strength. The word is applied to various engines, as a machine for driving piles, another for raising weights, and a machine for separating the seeds from cotton, invented by E. Whitney, is called a cotton-gin. It is also the name given to an engine of torture, and to a pump moved by rotary sails.
To clear cotton of its seeds by a machine which separates them with expedition.
ginger: A plant, or the root of a species of Amomum, a native of the East and West Indies. The roots are jointed, and the stalks rise two or three feet, with narrow leaves. The flower stems arise by the side of these, immediately from the root, naked and ending in an oblong scaly spike. The dried roots are used for various purposes, in the kitchen and in medicine.
gingerbreaad: A kind or cake, composed of flour with an admixture of butter, pearlash and ginger
gingham: A kind or striped cotton cloth.
ginging: In mining, the lining of a mine-shaft with stones or bricks for its support, called steining or staining, which I suppose is from Sax. stan
gingle: To make a sharp clattering sound; to ring as a little bell, or as small pieces of sonorous metal; as gingling halfpence.
To utter affected or chiming sounds in periods or cadence.
To shake so as to make clattering sounds in quick succession; to ring, as a little bell, or as small coins. The bells she gingled, and the whistle blew.
A shrill clattering sound, or a succession of sharp sounds, as those made by a little bell or by small coins.
Affection in the sounds of periods in reading or speaking, or rather chiming sounds.
ginglymoid: Pertaining to or resembling a ginglymus.
ginglymus: In anatomy, a species of articulation resembling a hinge. That species of articulation in which each bone partly receives and is partly received by the other, so as to admit only of flexion and extension, is called angular ginglymus.
ginseng: A plant, of the genus Panax, the root of which is in great demand among the Chinese. It is found in the Northern parts of Asia and America, and is an article of export from America to China. It has a jointed, fleshy, taper root, as large as a man’s finger, which when dry is of a yellowish white color, with a mucilaginous sweetness in the taste, somewhat resembling that of liquorice, accompanied with a slight bitterness.
gip: To take out the entrails of herrings.
gipsey: The Gipseys are a race of vagabonds which infest Europe, Africa and Asia, strolling about and subsisting mostly by theft, robbery and fortune-telling. The name is supposed to be corrupted from Egyptian, as they were thought to have come from Egypt. But their language indicates that they originated in Hindoostan.
A reproachful name for a dark complexion.
A name of slight reproach to a woman; sometimes implying artifice or cunning. A slave I am to Clara’s eyes: . The gipsey knows her power and flies.
gipseyism: The arts and practices of gipseys; deception; cheating; flattery.
girandole: A chandelier; a large kind of branched candlestick.
girasol: The turnsole, a plant of the genus Heliotropium.

A mineral usually milk white, bluish white or sky blue, but when turned towards the sun or any bright light, it constantly reflects a reddish color; hence its name. It sometimes resembles a translucid jelly .
gird: A twitch or pang; a sudden spasm, which resembles the stroke of a rod or the pressure of a band.
In popular language, a severe stroke of a stick or whip.
gurd. pret. and pp. girded or girt.
To bind by surrounding with any flexible substance, as with a twig, a cord, bandage or cloth; as, to gird the loins with sackcloth.
To make fast by binding; to put on; usually with on; as, to gird on a harness; to gird on a sword.
girder: In architecture, the principal piece of timber in a floor. Its end is usually fastened into the summers or breast summers, and the joists are framed in it at one end. In buildings entirely of timber, the girder is fastened by tenons into the posts.
girdle: A band or belt; something drawn round the waist of a person, and tied or buckled; as a girdle of fine lines; a leathern girdle.
Among jewelers, the line which encompasses the stone, parallel to the horizon.
girdle-belt: A belt that encircles the waist.
girdler: One who girdles; a maker of girdles.
girdle-stead: The part of the body where the girdle is worn.
girl: A female child, or young woman. In familiar language, any young unmarried woman.
Among sportsmen, a roebuck of two years old.
girlish: Like a young woman or child; befitting a girl.
Pertaining to the youth of a female.
girlishly: In the manner of a girl.
girrock: A species of gar-fish, the lacertus.
girt: The band or strap by which a saddle or any burden on a horse’s back is made fast, by passing under his belly.
The compass measured by a firth or inclosing bandage. He’s a lusty, jolly fellow, that lives well, at least three yards in the girth.
girth: The band or strap by which a saddle or any burden on a horse’s back is made fast, by passing under his belly.
The compass measured by a firth or inclosing bandage. He’s a lusty, jolly fellow, that lives well, at least three yards in the girth.
gist: In law, the main point of a question; the point on which an action rests.
give: To bestow; to confer; to pass or transfer the title or property of a thing to another person’ without an equivalent or compensation. For generous lords had rather give than pay.
To transmit from himself to another by hand, speech or writing; to deliver. The woman whom thou gavest to be with me, she gave me of the tree, and I did eat. Genesis 3:12.
To import; to bestow. Give us of your oil, for our lamps are gone out. Vatihew 25:8.
To communicate; as, to give an opinion; to give counsel or advice; to give notice.&
given: giv’n. Bestowed; granted; conferred; imparted; admitted or supposed.
giver: One who gives a donor; a bestower; a grantor; one who imparts or distributes. It is the giver, and not the gift, that engrosses the heart of the christian.
gives: Fetters or shackles for the feet.
giving: An alleging of what is not real.
gizzard: The strong musculus stomach of a fowl. To fret the gizzard, to harass; to vex one’s self, or to be vexed.
glaciation: The act of freezing; ice formed.
glacier: A field or immense mass of ice, formed in deep but elevated valleys, or on the sides of the Alps or other mountains. These masses of ice extend many miles in length and breadth, and remain undissolved by the heat of summer.
glacis: In building, or gardening, an easy, insensible slope.
In fortification, a sloping bank; that mass of earth which serves as a parapet to the covered way, having an easy slope or declivity towards the champaign or field.
glad: Pleased; affected with pleasure or moderate joy; moderately happy. A wise son maketh a glad father. Proverbs 10:7. It is usually followed by of. I am glad of an opportunity to oblige my friend. It is sometimes followed by at. He that is glad at calamities shall not be unpunished. Proverbs 17:5. It is sometimes followed by with. The Trojan, glad with sight of hostile blood-- . With, after glad, is unusual, and in this passage at would have been preferable.
Cheerful; joyous. They blessed the king, and went to their tents, joyful and glad of heart. 7 Kings 8:66.
Cheerful; wearing the appearance of joy; as a glad countenance.
Wearing a gay appearance; showy; bright. The wilderness and the solitary place shall be glad for them. Isaiah 35:1. Glad evening and glad morn crown’d the fourth day.
Pleasing; exhilarating. Her conversation . More glad to me than to a miser money is.
gladden: glad’n. To make glad; to cheer; to please; to exhilarate. The news of peace gladdens our hearts. Churches will every where gladden his eye, and hymns of praise vibrate upon his ear.
glad’n. To become glad; to rejoice. So shall your country ever gladden at the sound of your voice.
gladder: One that makes glad, or gives joy.
glade: An opening or passage made through a wood by lopping off the branches of the trees. Locally, in the U. States, a natural opening or open place in a forest. There interspersed in lawns and opening glades.
In New England, an opening in the ice of rivers or lakes, or a place left unfrozen.
gladen: Swordgrass; the general name of plants that rise with a broad blade like sedge.
glader: Swordgrass; the general name of plants that rise with a broad blade like sedge.
gladiate: Sword-shaped; resembling the form of a sword; as the legume of a plant.
gladiator: A sword-player; a prize-fighter. The gladiators, in Rome, were men who fought in the arena, for the entertainment of the people.
gladiatorial: Pertaining to gladiators, or to combats for the entertainment of the Roman people.
gladiole: A plant, the sword-lily, of the genus Gladiolus. The water gladiole is of the genus Butomus or flowering rush, and also of the genus Lobelia or cardinal flower.
gladly: With pleasure; joyfully; cheerfully. The common people heard him gladly. Mark 12:37.
gladness: Joy, or a moderate degree of joy and exhilaration; pleasure of mind; cheerfulness. They--did eat their meat with gladness and singleness of heart. Acis 2:46. [Gladness is rarely or never equivalent to mirth, merriment, gayety and triumph, and it usually expresses less than delight. It sometimes expresses great joy. Esther 8:15-17; Esther 9:17- 19.,I .
gladsome: Causing joy, pleasure or cheerfulness; having the appearance of gayety; pleasing. Of opening heaven they sung, and gladsome day.
gladsomely: With joy; with pleasure of mind.
gladsomeness: Joy, or moderate joy; pleasure of mind.
gladwin: A plant of the genus Iris.
glair: The white of an egg. It is used as a varnish for preserving paintings.
Any viscous transparent substance, resembling the white of an egg.
To smear with the white of an egg; to varnish.
glairy: Like glair, or partaking of its qualities.
glance: To shoot or dart a ray of light or splendor.
To shoot or dart suddenly or obliquely; to cast for a moment; as, to glance the eye.
A sudden shoot of light or splendor.
A shoot or darting of sight; a rapid or momentary view or cast; a snatch of sight; as a sudden glance; a glance of the eye.
To fly off in an oblique direction; to dart aside. The arrow struck the shield and glanced. So we say, a glancing ball or shot.
glance-coal: Anthracite; a mineral composed chiefly of carbon.
glancing: Shooting; darting; casting suddenly; flying off obliquely.
glancingly: By glancing; in a glancing manner; transiently.
gland: In anatomy, a distinct soft body, formed by the convolution of a great number of vessels, either constituting a part of the lymphatic system, or destined to secrete some fluid from the blood. Glands have been divided into conglobate and conglomerate, from their structure; but a more proper division is into lymphatic and secretory. The former are found in the course of the lymphatic vessels, and are conglobate. The latter are of various structure. They include the mucous follicles, the conglomerate glands, properly so called, such as the parotid glands and the pancreas, the liver, kidneysThe term has also been applied to other bodies of a similar appearance, neither lymphatic nor secretory; such as the thymus and thyroid glands, whose use is not certainly known, certain portions of the brain, as the pineal and pituitary glands[See Conglobate and Conglomerate.I .
In botany, a gland or glandule is an excretory or secretory duct or vessel in a plant. Glands are found on the leaves, petioles, peduncles and stipules.
glanders: In farriery, the running of corrupt slimy matter from the nose of a horse.
glandiferous: Bearing acorns or other nuts; producing nuts or mast. The beech and the oak are glandiferous trees.
glandiform: In the shape of a gland or nut; resembling a gland.
glandular: Containing glands; consisting of glands; pertaining to glands.
glandulation: In botany, the situation and structure of the secretory vessels in plants. Glandulation respects the secretory vessels, which are either glandules, follicles or utricles.WWIMAD .
glandule: A small gland or secreting vessel.
glandulous: Containing glands; consisting of glands; pertaining to glands; resembling glands.
glare: A bright dazzling light; clear, brilliant luster or splendor, that dazzles the eyes. The frame of burnished steel that cast a glare.
A fierce, piercing look. --About them round, A lion now he stalks with fiery glare.
To shine with a clear, bright, dazzling light; as glaring light. The cavern glares with new admitted light.
To look with fierce, piercing eyes. They glared, like angry lions.
glareous: Resembling the white of an egg; viscous and transparent or white.
glaring: Emitting a clear and brilliant light; shining with dazzling luster.
Clear; notorious; open and bold; barefaced; as a glaring crime.
glass: To see as in a glass.

Ahard, brittle, transparent, factitious substance, formed by fusing sand with fixed alkalies. In chimistry, a substance or mixture, earthy, saline or metallic, brought by fusion to the state of a hard, brittle, transparent mass, whose fracture is conchoidal.
A glass vessel of any kind; as a drinking glass.
A vessel to be filled with sand for measuring time; as an hour- glass.
glassblower: One whose business is to blow and fashion glass.
glassfull: As much as a glass holds.
glassfurnace: A furnace in which the materials of glass are melted.
glass-gazing: Addicted to viewing one’s self in a glass or mirror; finical.
glassgrinder: One whose occupation is to grind and polish glass.
glasshouse: A house where glass is made.
glassiness: The quality of being glassy or smooth; a vitreous appearance.
glasspot: A vessel used for melting glass in manufactories.
glassworks: The place or buildings where glass is made.
glasswort: A plant, the Salsola, of several species, all which may be used in the manufacture of glass. The Barilla of commerce, is the semifused ashes of the Salsola soda, which is largely cultivated on the Mediterranean in Spain.
glassy: Made of glass; vitreous; as a glassy substance.
Resembling glass in its properties, as in smoothness, brittleness, or transparency; as a glassy stream; a glassy surface; the glassy deep.
glauberite: A mineral of a grayish white or yellowish color, consisting of dry sulphate of lime and dry sulphate of soda.
glauber-salt: Sulphate of soda, a well known cathartic.
glaucoma: A fault in the eye, in which the crystalline humor becomes gray, but without injury to the sight. A disease in the eye, in which the crystalline humor becomes of a bluish or greenish color, and its transparency is diminished. An opacity of the vitreous humor. According to Sharp, the glaucoma of the Greeks is the same as the cataract; and according to St. Yves and others, it is a cataract with amaurosis.
glaucous: Of a sea green color; of a light green.
glaze: To furnish with windows of glass; as, to glaze a house.
To incrust with a vitreous substance, the basis of which is lead, but combined with silex, pearl-ashes and common salt; as, to glaze earthen ware.
To cover with any thing smooth and shining; or to render the exterior of a thing smooth, bright and showy. Though with other ornaments he may glaze and brandish the weapons.
To give a glass surface; to make glossy; as, to glaze cloth.
glazed: Furnished with glass windows; incrusted with a substance resembling glass; rendered smooth and shining.
glazier: gla’zhur. One whose business is to set window glass, or to fix panes of glass to the sashes of windows, to pictures.
glazing: Crusting with a vitreous substance, as potter’s ware.
Giving a smooth, glossy, shining surface, as to cloth.
The vitreous substance with which potter’s ware is incrusted.
gleam: The radical sense is to throw, to shoot or dart, and it may be of the same family as clamo, clamor, a shoot of the voice.
A shoot of light; a beam; a ray; a small stream of light. A gleam of dawning light, metaphorically, a gleam of hope.
Brightness; splendor. In the clear azure gleam the flocks are seen.
To shoot or dart, as rays of light. At the dawn light gleams in the east.
gleaming: Shooting as rays of light; shining.
A shoot or shooting of light.
gleamy: Darting beams of light; casting light in rays. In brazen arms, that cast a gleamy ray, Swift through the town the warrior bends his way.
glean: To gather the stalks and ears of grain which reapers leave behind them. Let me now go to the field, and glean ears of corn Ruth 2:2.

To collect things thinly scattered; to gather what is left in small parcels or numbers, or what is found in detached parcels; as, to glean a few passages from an author. They gleaned of them in the highways five thousand men. Judges 20:45.
To gather stalks or ears of grain left by reapers. And she went, and came and gleaned in the field after the reapers. Ruth 2:3.
A collection made by gleaning, or by gathering here and there a little. The gleans of yellow thyme distend his thighs.
gleaned: Gathered after reapers; collected from small detached parcels; as grain gleaned from the field.
Cleared of what is left; as, the field is gleaned.
Having suffered a gleaning. The public prints have been gleaned.
gleaner: One who collects detached parts or numbers, or who gathers slowly with labor.
gleaning: Gathering what reapers leave; collecting in small detached parcels.
The act of gathering after reapers.
That which is collected by gleaning.
glebe: Turf; soil; ground. Till the glad summons of a genial ray . Unbinds the glebe--- .
The land belonging to a parish church or ecclesiastical benefice.
Among miners, a piece of earth in which is contained some mineral ore.
glede: A fowl of the rapacious kind, the kite, a species of Falco. The word is used in Deuteronomy 14:13. but the same Hebrew word, Leviticus 11:14. is rendered a vulture.
glee: Joy; merriment; mirth; gayety; particularly, the mirth enjoyed at a feast.
A sort of catch or song sung in parts.
gleek: To make sport of; to gibe; to sneer; to spend time idly.
gleet: The flux of a thin humor from the urethra; a thin ichor running from a sore.
To flow in a thin limpid humor; to ooze.
glen: A valley; a dale; a depression or space between hills.
glene: In anatomy, the cavity or socket of the eye, and the pupil; any slight depression or cavity receiving a bone in articulation.
gliadine: One of the constituents of gluten, a slightly transparent, brittle substance, of a straw-yellow color, having a slight smell, similar to that of honeycomb.
glib: Smooth; slippery; admitting a body to slide easily on the surface; as, ice is glib.
Smooth; voluble; easily moving; as a glib tongue.
A thick curled bush of hair hanging down over the eyes.
glibly: Smoothly; volubly; as, to slide glibly; to speak glibly.
glibness: Smoothness; slipperiness; as a polished ice-like glibness.
glide: To flow gently; to move without noise or violence; as a river. By east, among the dusty vallies glide . The silver streams of Jordan’s crystal flood.
To move silently and smoothly; to pass along without apparent effort; as a hawk or an eagle gliding through the air.
To move or pass rapidly and with apparent ease; as, a ship glides through the water.
In a general sense, to move or slip along with ease as on a smooth surface, or to pass along rapidly without apparent effort, and without obstruction.
The act or manner of moving smoothly, swiftly and without labor or obstruction.
gliding: Passing along gently and smoothly; moving rapidly, or with ease.
glimmer: To shoot feeble or scattered rays of light; as the glimmering dawn; a glimmering lamp. When rosy morning glimmer’d o’er the dales. The west yet glimmers with some streaks of day.
To shine faintly; to give a feeble light. Mild evening glimmered on the lawn.
A faint light; feeble scattered rays of light.

In mineralogy, mica, glist, muscovy-glass; a mineral resulting from crystallization, but rarely found in regular crystals. usually it appears in thin, flexible, elastic lamins, which exhibit a high polish and strong luster. It is an essential ingredient in granite, gneiss, and mica slate.
glimmering: Shining faintly; shooting feeble scattered rays of light.
glimpse: A weak faint light. Such vast room in Nature,IVM . Only to shine, yet scarce to contribute . Each orb a glimpse of light.
A flash of light; as the lightning’s glimpse.
Transient luster. One glimpse of glory to my issue give.
A short transitory view. He saw at a glimpse the design of the enemy.
Short fleeting enjoyment; as a glimpse of delight.
glissa: A fish of the tunny kind, without scales.
glisten: glis’n. To shine; to sparkle with light; as the glistening stars. The ladies’ eyes glistened with pleasure.
glistening: Shining; sparkling; emitting rays of light.
glister: To shine; to be bright; to sparkle; to be brilliant. All that glistens is not gold.
glitter: To shine; to sparkle with light; to gleam; to be splendid; as a glittering sword. The field yet glitters with the pomp of war.
To be showing, specious or striking, and hence attractive; as the glittering scenes of a court.
Brightness; brilliancy; splendor; luster; as the glitter of arms; the glitter of royal equipage; the glitter of dress.
gloat: To cast side glances; to stare with eagerness or admiration.
globate: Having the form of a globe; spherical; spheroidal.
globated: Having the form of a globe; spherical; spheroidal.
globe: A round or spherical solid body; a ball; a sphere; a body whose surface is in every part equidistant from the center.
The earth; the terraqueous ball; so called, though not perfectly spherical.

An artificial sphere of metal, paper or other matter, on whose convex surface is drawn a map or representation of the earth or of the heavens. That on which the several oceans, seas, continents, isles and countries of the earth are represented, is called a terrestrial globe. That which exhibits a delineation of the constellations in the heavens, is called a celestial globe.
A body of soldiers formed into a circle.
To gather round or into a circle.
globe-amaranth: A plant of the genus Gomphrena.
globe-animal: A species of animalcule of a globular form.
globe-daisy: A plant or flower of the genus Globularia.
globe-fish: A fish of a globular shape, the Ostracion.
globe-flower: A plant or flower of the genus Sphaeranthus.
globe-thistle: A plant of the genus Echinops.
globosity: The quality of being round; sphericity.
globular: Round; spherical; having the form of a small ball or sphere; as globular atoms.
globule: A little globe; a small particle of matter of a spherical form; a word particularly applied to the red particles of blood, which swim in a transparent serum, and may be discovered by the microscope. Hail stones have opake globules of snow in their center.
globulous: Round; globular; having the form of a small sphere.
glome: In botany, a roundish head of flowers.
glomerate: To gather or wind into a ball; to collect into a spherical form or mass, as threads.
glomerated: Gathered into a ball or round mass.
glomerating: Collecting or winding into a ball or round mass.
glomeration: The act of gathering, winding or forming into a ball or spherical body.
A body formed into a ball.
glomerous: Gathered or formed into a ball or round mass.
gloom: Obscurity; partial or total darkness; thick shade; as the gloom of a forest, or the gloom of midnight.
Cloudiness or heaviness of mind; melancholy; aspect of sorrow. We say, the mind is sunk into gloom; a gloom overspreads the mind.
gloomily: With melancholy aspect; sullenly; not cheerfully.
gloominess: Want of light; obscurity; darkness; dismalness.
Want of cheerfulness; cloudiness of look; heaviness of mind; melancholy; as, to involve the mind in gloominess.
gloomy: Obscure; imperfectly illuminated; or dark; dismal; as the gloomy cells of a convent; the gloomy shades of night.
Wearing the aspect of sorrow; melancholy; clouded; dejected; depressed; heavy of heart; as a gloomy countenance or state of mind; a gloomy temper.
glorification: The act of giving glory or of ascribing honors to.
Exaltation to honor and dignity; elevation to glory; as the glorification of Christ after his resurrection.
glorify: To praise; to magnify and honor in worship; to ascribe honor to, in thought or words. Psalm 86:9 . God is glorified, when such his excellency, above all things, is with due admiration acknowledged.
To make glorious; to exalt to glory, or to celestial happiness. Whom he justified, them he also glorified. Romans 8:30. The God of our fathers hath glorified his son Jesus. Acts 3:13.
To praise; to honor; to extol. Whomsoever they find to be most licentious of life--him they set up and glorify.
To procure honor or praise to.
glorifying: Praising; honoring in worship; exalting to glory; honoring; extolling.
glorious: Illustrious; of exalted excellence and splendor; resplendent in majesty and divine attributes; applied to God. Exodus 15:11.
Noble; excellent; renowned; celebrated; illustrious; very honorable; applied to men, their achievements, titles. Let us remember we are Cato’s friends, And act like men who claim that glorious title.
gloriously: Splendidly; illustriously; with great renown or dignity. Sing ye to the Lord, for he hath triumphed gloriously. Exodus 15:21.
glory: Brightness; luster; splendor. The moon, serene in glory, mounts the sky. For he received from God the Father honor and glory, when there came such a voice to him from the excellent glory. 2 Peter 1:17. In this passage of Peter, the latter word glory refers to the visible splendor or bright cloud that overshadowed Christ at his transfiguration. The former word glory, though the same in the original, is to be understood in a figurative sense.
Splendor; magnificence. Solomon, in all his glory, was not arrayed like one of these. Matthew 6:29.
The circle of rays surrounding the head of a figure in painting.&
Praise ascribed in adoration; honor. Glory to God in the highest. Luke 2:14.
Honor; praise; fame; renown; celebrity. The hero pants for glory in the field. It was the glory of Howard to relieve the wretched.
glorying: The act of exulting; exultation; boasting; display of pride. Your glorying is not good. 7 Corinthians 5:6.
gloss: Brightness or luster of a body proceeding from a smooth surface; as the gloss of silk; cloth is calendared to give it a gloss.
A specious appearance or representation; external show that may mislead opinion. It is no part of my secret meaning to set on the face of this cause any fairer gloss than the naked truth doth afford.
Interpretation; comment; explanation; remark intended to illustrate a subject. All this, without a gloss or comment, He would unriddle in a moment. Explaining the text in short glosses.
glossarist: A writer of glosses or comments.
glossary: A dictionary or vocabulary, explaining obscure or antiquated words found in old authors; such as Du Canage’s Glossary; Spelman’s Glossary.
glossator: A writer of comments; a commentator.
glosser: A writer of glosses; a scholiast; a commentator.
A polisher; one who gives a luster.
glossiness: The luster or brightness of a smooth surface.
glossing: Giving luster to; polishing; explaining by comments; giving a specious appearance.
glossographer: A writer of glosses; a commentator; a scholiast.
glossography: The writing of comments for illustrating an author.
glossologist: One who writes glosses; a commentator.
glossology: Glosses or commentaries; explanatory notes for illustrating an author.
glossy: Smooth and shining; reflecting luster from a smooth surface; highly polished; as glossy silk; a glossy raven; a glossy plum.
glottis: The narrow opening at the upper part of the aspera arteria or windpipe, which, by its dilatation and contraction, contributes to the modulation of the voice.
glove: A cover for the hand, or for the hand and arm, with a separate sheath for each finger. The latter circumstance distinguishes the glove from the mitten. To throw the glove, with our ancestors, was to challenge to single combat.
glover: One whose occupation is to make and sell gloves.
glow: To shine with intense heat; or perhaps more correctly, to shine with a white heat; to exhibit incandescence. Hence, in a more general sense, to shine with a bright luster. Glows in the stars, and blossoms in the trees.
To burn with vehement heat. The scorching fire that in their entrails glows.
To feel great heat of body; to be hot. Did not his temples glow . In the same sultry winds and scorching heats?
To exhibit a strong bright color; to be red. Clad in a gown that glows with Tyrian rays. Fair ideas flow, Strike in the sketch, or in the picture glow.
To be bright or red with heat or animation, or with blushes; as glowing cheeks.
glowing: Shining with intense heat; white with heat.
Exhibiting a bright color; red; as a glowing color; glowing cheeks.
Ardent; vehement; animated; as glowing zeal.
glowingly: With great brightness; with ardent heat or passion.
glowworm: The female of the Lampyris noctiluca, an insect of the order of Coleopters. It is without wings and resembles a caterpillar. It emits a shining green light from the extremity of the abdomen. The male is winged and flies about in the evening, when it is attracted by the light of the female.
gloze: To flatter; to wheedle; to fawn; that is, to smooth, or to talk smoothly. So glozed the tempter, and his proem tun’d. A false glozing parasite.
glucin: A soft white earth or powder obtained from the beryl and emerald; so named from its forming, with acids, salts that are sweet to the taste. Glucin is a compound, of which glucinum is the base.
glue: Inspissated animal gluten; a tenacious, viscid matter, which serves as a cement to unite other substances. It is made of the skins, paringsof animals, as of oxen, calves or sheep, by boiling them to a jelly; .
To join with glue or a viscous substance. Cabinet makers glue together some parts of furniture.
To unite; to hold together. J .
glueboiler: One whose occupation is to make glue.
glum: Sullenness; and, as a verb, to look sullen.
glume: In botany, the calyx or corol of corn and grasses, formed of valves embracing the seed, often terminated by the arista or beard; the husk or chaff.
glumous: A glumous flower is a kind of aggregate flower, having a filiform receptacle, with a common glume at the base.
glut: To swallow, or to swallow greedily; to gorge.
To Cloy; to fill beyond sufficiency; to sate; to disgust; as, to glut the appetites.
To feast or delight even to satiety. His faithful heart, a bloody sacrifice, Torn from his breast, to glut the tyrant’s eyes.
To fill or furnish beyond sufficiency; as, to glut the market.
gluteal: The gluteal artery, is a branch of the hypogastric or internal iliac artery, which supplies the gluteal muscles. The gluteal muscles, are three large muscles on each side, which make up the fleshy part of the buttocks.
gluten: A tough elastic substance, of a grayish color, which becomes brown and brittle by drying; found in the flour of wheat and other grain. It contributes much to the nutritive quality of flour, and gives tenacity to its paste. A similar substance is found in the juices of certain plants.
That part of the blood which gives firmness to its texture.
glutinate: To unite with glue; to cement.
glutination: The act of uniting with glue.
glutinative: Having the quality of cementing; tenacious.
glutinosity: The quality of being glutinous; viscousness.
glutinous: Viscous; viscid; tenacious; having the quality of glue; resembling glue. Starch is glutinous.
In botany, besmeared with a slippery moisture; as a glutinous leaf.
glutinousness: Viscosity; viscidity; the quality of glue
glutton: glut’n. One who indulges to excess in eating.
One eager of any thing to excess. Gluttons in murder, wanton to destroy.

In zoology, an animal of the genus Ursus, found in the N. of Europe and Siberia. It grows to the length of three feet, but has short legs and moves slowly. It is a carnivorous animal, and in order to catch its prey, it climbs a tree and from that darts down upon a deer or other animal. It is names from its voracious appetite.
gluttonize: To eat to excess; to eat voraciously; to indulge the appetite to excess; to be luxurious.
gluttonous: Given to excessive eating; indulging the appetite for food to excess; as a gluttonous age.
Consisting in excessive eating; as gluttonous delight.
gluttonously: With the voracity of a glutton; with excessive eating.
gluttony: Excess in eating; extravagant indulgence of the appetite for food.
Luxury of the table: . Their sumptuous’ gluttonies and gorgeous feasts.
glyconian: Denoting a kind of verse in Greek and Latin poetry, consisting of three feet, a spondee, a choriamb, and a pyrrhic; as Glyconic measure.
glyconic: Denoting a kind of verse in Greek and Latin poetry, consisting of three feet, a spondee, a choriamb, and a pyrrhic; as Glyconic measure.
glyph: In sculpture and architecture, a canal, channel or cavity intended as an ornament.
glyphic: A picture or figure by which a word is implied.
glyptic: The art of engraving figures on precious stones.
glyptographic: Describing the methods of engraving on precious stones.
glyptograpny: A description of the art of engraving on precious stones.
gnar: nar and n’arl. To growl; to murmur; to snarl.
n‘ar and narl. To growl; to murmur; to snarl. And wolves are gnarling which shall gnaw thee first.
gnarl: nar and n’arl. To growl; to murmur; to snarl.
n‘ar and narl. To growl; to murmur; to snarl. And wolves are gnarling which shall gnaw thee first.
gnarled: n’arled. Knotty; full of knots; as the gnarled oak.
gnash: nash. To strike the teeth together, as in anger or pain; as, to gnash the teeth in rage.
nash. To grind the teeth. He shall gnash with his teeth and melt away. Psalm 112:10.
To rage even to collision with the teeth; to growl. They gnashed on me with their teeth. Psalm 35:16.
gnashing: nash’ing. Striking the teeth together, as in anger, rage or pain.
nash’ing. A grinding or striking of the teeth in rage or anguish. There shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth. Matthew 8:12.
gnat: A small insect, or rather a genus of insects, the Culex, whose long cylindric body is composed of eight rings. They have six legs and their mouth is formed by a flexible sheath, inclosing bristles pointed like stings. The sting is a tube containing five or six spicula of exquisite fineness, dentated or edged. The most troublesome of this genus is the musketoe.
Any thing proverbially small. Ye blind guides, who strain at a gnat, and swallow a camel. Matthew 23:24.
gnatworm: A small water insect produced by a gnat, and which after its several changes is transformed into a gnat; the larva of a gnat.
gnaw: To bite off by little and little; to bite or scrape off with the fore teeth; to wear away by biting. The rats gnaw a board or plank; a worm gnaws the wood of a tree or the plank of a ship.
To eat by biting off small portions of food with the fore teeth.
To bite in agony or rage. They gnawed their tongues for pain. Revelation 16:10.
To waste; to fret; to corrode.
gnawer: naw’er. He or that which gnaws or corrodes.
gnawing: naw’ing. Biting off by little and little; corroding; eating by slow degrees.
gneiss: ne’is. In mineralogy, a species of aggregated rock, composed of quartz, feldspar and mica, of a structure more or less distinctly slaty. The layers, whether straight or curved, are frequently thick, but often vary considerably in the same specimen. It passes on one side into granite, from which it differs in its slaty structure, and on the other into mica slate. It is rich in metalic ores.
gnome: An imaginary being, supposed by the cabalists, to inhabit the inner parts of the earth, and to be the guardian of mines
gnomiometrical: The gnomiometrical telescope and microscope is an instrument for measuring the angles of crystals by reflection, and for ascertaining the inclination of strata, and the apparaent magnitude of angles when the eye is not’ placed at the vertex.
gnomology: A collection of maxims, grave sentences or reflections.
gnomon: In dialling, the style or pin, which by its shadow shows the hour of the day. It represents the axis of the earth.
In astronomy, a style erected perpendicular to the horizon, in order to find the altitude of the sun.
The gnomon of a globe, is the index of the hour-circle.
gnomonic: Pertaining to the art of dialling.
gnomonical: Pertaining to the art of dialling.
gnomonics: The art or science of dialling, or of constructing the dials to show the hour of the day by the shadow of a gnomon.
gnostic: The Gnostics were a sect of philosophers that arose in the first ages of christianity, who pretended they were the only men who had a true knowledge of the christian religion. They formed for themselves a system of theology, agreeable to the philosophy of Pythagoras and Plato, to which they accommodated their interpretations of scripture. They held that all natures, intelligible, intellectual and material, are derived by successive emanations from the infinite fountain of deity. These emanations they called oeons. These doctrines were derived from the oriental philosophy.
Pertaining to the Gnostics or their doctrines.
gnosticism: nos’ticism. The doctrines, principles or systems of philosophy taught by the Gnostics.
gnu: A species of Antelope, in Southern Africa, whose form partakes of that of the horse, the ox, and the deer.
go: In a general sense, to move; to pass; to proceed from one place, state or station to another; opposed to resting. A mill goes by water or by steam; a ship goes at the rate of five knots an hour; a clock goes fast or slow; a horse goes lame; a fowl or a ball goes with velocity through the air. The mourners go about the streets. Ecclesiastes 12:5.
To walk; to move on the feet or step by step. The child begins to go alone at a year old. You know that love . Will creep in service where it cannot go.
To walk leisurely; not to run. Thou must run to him; for thou hast staid so long that going will scarce serve the turn.
To travel; to journey by land or water. I must go to Boston. He has gone to Philadelphia. The minister is going to France .
go-between: An interposer; one who transacts business between parties.
go-by: A passing without notice; a thrusting away; a shifting off.
go-cart: A machine with wheels, in which children learn to walk without danger of falling.
goad: A pointed instrument used to stimulate a beast to move faster.
To prick; to drive with a goad.

To incite; to stimulate; to instigate; to urge forward, or to rouse by any thing pungent, severe, irritating or inflaming. He was goaded by sarcastic remarks or by abuse; goaded by desire or other passion.
goaded: Pricked; pushed on by a goad; instigated.
goading: Pricking; driving with a goad; inciting; urging on; rousing.
goal: The point set to bound a race, and to which they run; the mark. Part curb their fiery steeds, or shun the goal . With rapid wheels./ .
The end or final purpose; the end to which a design tends, or which a person aims to reach or accomplish. Each individual seeks a several goal.
goat: An animal or quadruped of the genus Capra. The horns are hollow, turned upwards, erect and scabrous. Goats are nearly of the size of sheep, but stronger, less timid and more agile. They delight to frequent rocks and mountains, and subsist on scanty coarse food. The milk of the goat is sweet, nourishing and medicinal, and the flesh furnishes provisions to the inhabitants of countries where they abound.
goat-chaffer: An insect, a kind of beetle.
goatherd: One whose occupation is to tend goats.
goatish: Resembling a goat in any quality; of a rank smell.
goat-milker: A kind or owl, so called from sucking goats.
goat-sucker: In ornithology, a fowl of the genus Caprimulgus, so called from the opinion that it would suck goats. It is called also the fern-owl. In Bailey, it is called a goat- milker.
gob: A little mass or collection; a mouthful.
gobbet: To swallow in large masses or mouthfuls.
gobble: To swallow in large pieces; to swallow hastily.
To make a noise in the throat, as a turkey.
gobbler: One who swallows in haste; a greedy eater; a gormandizer.
Aname sometimes given to the turkey cock.
goblet: A kind of cup or drinking vessel without a handle. We love not loaded boards, and goblets crown’d.
goblin: An evil spirit; a walking spirit; a frightful phantom. To whom the goblin, full of wrath.
god: The Supreme Being; Jehovah; the eternal and infinite spirit, the creator, and the sovereign of the universe. God is a spirit; and they that worship him, must worship him in spirit and in truth. John 4:24.
A false god; a heathen deity; an idol. Fear not the gods of the Amorites. Judges 6:10.
A prince; a ruler; a magistrate or judge; an angel. Thou shalt not revile the gods, nor curse the ruler of thy people. Exodus 22:28; Psalm 97:7, 9.
Any person or thing exalted too much in estimation, or deified and honored as the chief good. Whose god is their belly. Philippians 3:19.
godchild: One for whom a person becomes sponsor at baptism, and promises to see educated as a christian.
goddaughter: A female for whom one becomes sponsor at baptism.
goddess: A female deity; a heathen deity of the female sex. When the daughter of Jupiter presented herself among a crowd of goddesses, she was distinguished by her graceful stature and superior beauty.
In the language of love, a woman of superior charms or excellence.
godfather: The man who is sponsor for a child at baptism, who promises to answer for his future conduct and that he shall follow a life of piety, by this means laying himself under an indispensable obligation to instruct the child and watch over his conduct. This practice is of high antiquity in the christian church, and was probably intended to prevent children from being brought up in idolatry, in case the parents died before the children had arrived to years of discretion. In the catholic church the number of godfathers and godmothers is reduced to two; in the church of England, to three; but formerly the number was not limited.
To act as godfather; to take under one’s fostering care.
godhead: Godship; deity; divinity; divine nature or essence; applied to the true God, and to heathen deities.
A deity in person; a god or goddess.
godless: Having no reverence for God; impious; ungodly; irreligious; wicked.
Atheistical; having no belief in the existence of God.
godlessness: The state of being impious or irreligious.
godlike: Resembling a deity, or heathen divinity.
Of superior excellence; as godlike virtue; a godlike prince.
godliness: Piety; belief in God, and reverence for his character and laws.

A religious life; a careful observance of the laws of God and performance of religious duties, proceeding from love and reverence for the divine character and commands; christian obedience. Godliness is profitable unto all things.7 Timothy 4:8.
Revelation; the system of christianity. Without controversy, great is the mystery of godliness; . God was manifest in the flesh.7 Timothy 3:16.
godling: A little deity; a diminutive god; as a puny godling.
godly: Pious; reverencing God, and his character and laws.
Living in obedience to God’s commands, from a principle of love to him and reverence of his character and precepts; religious; righteous; as a godly person.
Pious; conformed to God’s law; as a godly life.
Piously; righteously. All that will live godly in Christ Jesus shall suffer persecution. Timothy 3:12.
godmother: A woman who becomes sponsor for a child in baptism.
godship: Deity; divinity; the rank or character of a god. O’er hills and dales their godships came.
godson: One for whom another has been sponsor at the font.
god speed: God speed, that is success. 2 John 10.
godwit: A fowl of the grallic order and genus Scolopax. It has a bill four inches long; the feathers on the head, neck and back are of a light reddish brown; those on the belly white, and the tail is regularly barred with black and white. This fowl frequents fens and the banks of rivers, and its flesh is esteemed a great delicacy.
goer: One that goes; a runner or walker; one that has a gait good or bad.
One that transacts business between parties; in an ill sense.
A term applied to a horse; as a good goer; a safe goer.
goff: A foolish clown; also, a game.
goggle: To strain or roll the eyes. And wink and goggle like an owl.
A strained or affected rolling of the eye.
goggle-eyed: Having prominent, distorted or rolling eyes.
goggles: In surgery, instruments used to cure squinting, or the distortion of the eyes which occasions it.
Cylindrical tubes, in which are fixed glasses for defending the eyes from cold, dustand sometimes with colored glasses to abate the intensity of light.
Blinds for horses that are apt to take fright.
going: Moving; walking; traveling; turning; rolling; flying; sailing
The act of moving in any manner.
goiter: The bronchocele; a large tumor that forms gradually on the human throat between the trachea and the skin. The inhabitants of this part of the Valais are subject to goiters.
goitrous: Pertaining to the goiter; partaking of the nature of bronchocele.
Affected with bronchocele. Let me not be understood as insinuating that the inhabitants in general are either goitrous or idiots.
gola: In architecture, the same as cymatium.
An ogee or wavy member in a building; the cymatium.
gold: A precious metal of a bright yellow color, and the most ductile and malleable of all the metals. It is the heaviest metal except platina; and being a very dense, fixed substance, and not liable to be injured by air, it is well fitted to be used as coin, or a representative of commodities in commerce. Its ductility and malleability render it the most suitable metal for gilding. It is often found native in solid masses, as in Hungary and Peru; though generally in combination with silver, copper or iron.
Money. For me, the gold of France did not seduce-- .
Something pleasing or valuable; as a heart of gold.
A bright yellow color; as a flower edged with gold.
Riches; wealth. Gold of pleasure, a plant of the genus Myagrum.
goldbeater: One whose occupation is to beat or foliate gold for gilding. Goldbeater’s skin, the intestinum rectum of an ox, which goldbeaters lay between the leaves of the metal while they beat it, whereby the membrane is reduced very thin, and made fit to be applied to cuts and fresh wounds.
gold coast: In geography, the coast of Africa where gold is found; being a part of the coast of Guinea.
golden: Made of gold; consisting of gold.
Bright; shining; splendid; as the golden sun. Reclining soft on many a golden cloud.
Yellow; of a gold color; as a golden harvest; golden fruit.
Excellent; most valuable; as the golden rule.
Happy; pure; as the golden age, the age of simplicity and purity of manners.
golden-lungwort: A plant of the genus Hieracium.
goldenmaidenhair: A plant of the genus Polytrichum.
golden-mouseear: A plant of the genus Hieracium.
golden-thistle: A plant of the genus Scolymus.
goldfinch: The Fringilla carduelis, a bird so named from the color of its wings.
gold-finder: One who finds gold; one who empties jakes.
goldfish: A fish of the genus Cyprinus, of the size of a pilchard, so named from its bright color. These fishes are bred by the Chinese, in small ponds, in basons or porcelain vessels, and kept for ornament.
goldenfish: A fish of the genus Cyprinus, of the size of a pilchard, so named from its bright color. These fishes are bred by the Chinese, in small ponds, in basons or porcelain vessels, and kept for ornament.
goldleaf: Gold foliated or beaten into a thin leaf.
gold-pleasure: for gold of pleasure, a plant of the genus Myagrum.
gold-proof: Proof against bribery or temptation by money.
gold-size: A size or glue for burnishing gilding.
goldsmith: An artisan who manufactures vessels and ornaments of gold and silver.
A banker; one who manages the pecuniary concerns of others.
goldthread: A thread formed of flatted gold laid over a thread of silk, by twisting it with a wheel and iron bobbins.
A plant, the Helleborus trifolius; so called from its fibrous yellow roots.
goldwire: An ingot of silver, superficially covered with gold and drawn through small round holes.
goldylocks: A name given to certain plants of the genera Chrysocoma and Gnaphalium.
golf: A game with ball and bat, in which he who drives the ball into a hole with the fewest strokes is the winner.
goloe-shoe: An over-shoe; a shoe worn over another to keep the foot dry.
gondola: A flat-bottomed boat, very long and narrow, used at Venice in Italy on the canals. A gondola of middle size is about thirty feet long and four broad, terminating at each end in a sharp point or peak rising to the highth of a man. It is usually rowed by two men, called gondoliers, who propel the boat by pushing the oars. The gondola is also used in other parts of Italy for a passage boat.
gondolier: A man who rows a gondola.
gone: Departed. It was told Solomon that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath. 1 Kings 2:41.
Advanced; forward in progress; with far, farther, or further; as a man far gone in intemperance.
Ruined; undone. Exert yourselves, or we are gone.
Past; as, these happy days are gone; sometimes with by. Those times are gone by.
gong: An instrument made of brass, of a circular form, which the Asiatics strike with a wooden mallet.
goniometer: An instrument for measuring solid angles, or the inclination of planes.
goniometrical: Pertaining to a goniometer. Goniometrical lines are used for measuring the quantity of angles.
gonorrhea: A morbid discharge in venereal complaints.
good: Valid; legally firm; not weak or defective; having strength adequate to its support; as a good title; a good deed; a good claim.
Valid; sound; not weak, false or fallacious; as a good argument.

Complete or sufficiently perfect in its kind; having the physical qualities best adapted to its design and use; opposed to bad, imperfect, corruptedWe say, good timber, good cloth, a good soil, a good color. And God saw every thing that he had made, and behold, it was very good. Genesis 1:31.

Having moral qualities best adapted to its design and use, or the qualities which God’s law requires; virtuous; pious; religious; applied to persons, and opposed to bad, vitious, wicked. Yet peradventure for a good man some would even dare to die. Romans 5:7.
Conformable to the moral law; virtuous; applied to actions. In all things showing thyself a pattern of good works. Jifus 2:7.
good-breeding: Polite manners, formed by a good education; a polite education.
good-conditioned: Being in a good state; having good qualities or favorable symptoms.
good-fellow: To make a jolly companion; to besot.
good-friday: A fast of the christian church, in memory of our Savior’s sufferings, kept in passion week.
good-humor: A cheerful temper or state of mind.
good-humoredly: With a cheerful temper; in a cheerful way.
good-nature: Natural mildness and kindness of disposition.
good-natured: Naturally mild in temper; not easily provoked.
good-now: An exclamation of wonder or surprise.
good-speed: Good success; an old form of wishing success.
goodliness: Beauty of form; grace; elegance. Her goodliness was full of harmony to his eyes.
goodly: Being of a handsome form; beautiful; graceful; as a goodly person; goodly raiment; goodly houses.
Pleasant; agreeable; desirable; as goodly days.
goodman: A familiar appellation of civility; sometimes used ironically. With you, goodman boy, if you please.
A rustic term of compliment; as old goodman Dobson.
A familiar appellation of a husband; also, the master of a family. Proverbs 7:19; Matthew 24:43.
goodness: The state of being good; the physical qualities which constitute value, excellence or perfection; as the goodness of timber; the goodness of a soil.
The moral qualities which constitute christian excellence; moral virtue; religion. The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, long-suffering, gentleness, goodnessGalatians 5:22.
Kindness; benevolence; benignity of heart; but more generally, acts of kindness; charity; humanity exercised. I shall remember his goodness to me with gratitude.
Kindness; benevolence of nature; mercy. The Lord God--abundant in goodness and truth. Exodus 34:6.
Kindness; favor shown; acts of benevolence, compassion or mercy. Jethro rejoiced for all the goodness which Jehovah had done to Israel. Exodus 18:9.
goods: Personal or movable estate; as horses, cattle, utensils.
Wares; merchandize; commodities bought and sold by merchants and traders.
goody: A low term of civility; as goody Dobson.
goodyship: The state or quality of a goody.
googings: In seamen’s language, clamps of iron bolted on the stern- post of a ship, whereon to hang the rudder.
goodings: In seamen’s language, clamps of iron bolted on the stern- post of a ship, whereon to hang the rudder.
goom: A man recently married, or who is attending his proposed spouse for the purpose of marriage; used in composition, as in bridegoom. It has been corrupted into groom.
goosander: A migratory fowl of the genus Mergus, the diver or plunger; called also merganser.
goose: A well known aquatic fowl of the genus Anas; but the domestic goose lives chiefly on land, and feeds on grass. The soft feathers are used for beds, and the quills for pens. The wild goose is migratory.
A tailor’s smoothing iron, so called from its handle which resembles the neck of a goose.
gooseberry: goos’berry. The fruit of a shrub, and the shrub itself, the Ribes grossularia. The shrub is armed with spines. Of the fruit there are several varieties. The American gooseberry belongs to the genus Melastoma, and the West Indian, gooseberry to the genus Cactus.
goosegrass: goos’grass. A plant of the genus Galium. Also, the name of certain plants of the genera Potentilla and Asperugo.
gooseneck: goos’neck. In a ship, a piece of iron fixed on one end of the tiller, to which the laniard of the whip-staff or wheel-rope comes, for steering the ship; also, an iron hook on the inner end of a boom.
goosequill: goos’quill. The large feather or quill of a goose; or a pen made with it.
goosetongue: goos’tung. A plant of the genus Achillea.
goosewing: goos’wing. In seamen’s language, a sail set on a boom on the lee side of a ship; also, the clues or lower corners of a ship’s main-sail or fore-sail, when the middle part is furled.
gor-cock: The moor-cock, red-grouse, or red-game; a fowl of the gallinaceous kind.
gordian: Intricate. Gordian knot, in antiquity, a knot in the leather or harness of Gordius, a king of Phrygia, so very intricate, that there was no finding where it began or ended. An oracle declared that he who should untie this knot should be master of Asia. Alexander, fearing that his inability to untie it should prove an ill augury, cut it asunder with his sword. Hence, in modern language, a Gordian knot is an inextricable difficulty; and to cut the Gordian knot, is to remove a difficulty by bold or unusual measures.
gore: Blood; but generally, thick or clotted blood; blood that after effusion becomes inspissated.
A wedge-shaped or triangular piece of cloth sewed into a garment to widen it in any part.
A slip or triangular piece of land.
In heraldry, an abatement denoting a coward. It consists of two arch lines, meeting in an acute angle in the middle of the fess point.
gored: Stabbed; pierced with a pointed instrument.
gorge: The throat; the gullet; the canal of the neck by which food passes to the stomach.
In architecture, the narrowest part of the Tuscan and Doric capitals, between the astragal, above the shaft of the column, and the annulets.
In fortification, the entrance of the platform of any work.
That which is gorged or swallowed, especially by a hawk or other fowl.
gorj. To swallow; especially, to swallow with greediness, or in large mouthfuls or quantities. Hence,
gorged: In heraldry, bearing a crown or the like about the neck.
gorgeous: Showy; fine; splendid; glittering with gay colors. With gorgeous wings, the marks of sovereign sway. A gorgeous robe. Luke 23:11.
gorgeously: With showy magnificence; splendidly; finely. The prince was gorgeously arrayed.
gorgeousness: Show of dress or ornament; splendor of raiment.
gorget: A piece of armor for defending the throat or neck; a kind of breast-plate like a half-moon; also, a small convex ornament worn by officers on the breast.
Formerly, a ruff worn by females.
In surgery, gorget, or gorgeret, is a cutting instrument used in lithotomy; also, a concave or cannulated conductor, called a blunt gorget.
gorgon: A fabled monster of terrific aspect, the sight of which turned the beholder to stone. The poets represent the Gorgons as three sisters, Stheno, Euryale and Medusa; but authors are not agreed in the description of them.
Any thing very ugly or horrid.
Like a gorgon; very ugly or terrific; as a gorgon face.
gorgonean: Like a gorgon; pertaining to gorgons. Gorgonia nobilis, in natural history, red coral.
gorgonian: Like a gorgon; pertaining to gorgons. Gorgonia nobilis, in natural history, red coral.
gormand: A greedy or ravenous eater; a glutton.
gormander: A greedy or ravenous eater; a glutton.
gormandize: To eat greedily; to swallow voraciously.
gorse: Furz, or whin, a thick prickly shrub, of the genus Ulex, bearing yellow flowers in winter.
gorss: Furz, or whin, a thick prickly shrub, of the genus Ulex, bearing yellow flowers in winter.
gory: Covered with congealed or clotted blood; as gory locks.
goshawk: A voracious fowl of the genus Falco, or hawk kind, larger than the common buzzard, but of a more slender shape. The general color of the plumage is a deep brown; the breast and belly white.
gosling: A young goose; a goose not full grown.
A catkin on nut trees and pines.
gospel: The history of the birth, life, actions, death, resurrection, ascension and doctrines of Jesus Christ; or a revelation of the grace of God to fallen man through a mediator, including the character, actions, and doctrines of Christ, with the whole scheme of salvation, as revealed by Christ and his apostles. This gospel is said to have been preached to Abraham, by the promise, “in thee shall all nations be blessed.” Galatians 3:8. It is called the gospel of God. Romans 1:1. It is called the gospel of Christ. Romans 1:16. It is called the gospel of salvation. Ephesians 1:13.
To instruct in the gospel; or to fill with sentiments of religion.
gospel-gossip: One who is over zealous in running about among his neighbors to lecture on religious subjects.
gospelize: To form according to the gospel.
To instruct in the gospel; to evangelize; as, to gospelize the savages.
gospelizing: Evangelizing; instructing in the christian religion.
gospeller: An evangelist; also, a follower of Wickliffe, the first Englishman who attempted a reformation from popery.
He who reads the gospel at the altar.
goss: A kind of low furz or gorse.
gossamer: A fine filmy substance, like cobwebs, floating in the air, in calm clear weather, especially in autumn. It is seen in stubble fields and on furz or low bushes, and is probably formed by a species of spider.
gossip: A sponsor; one who answers for a child in baptism; a godfather.
A tippling companion. And sometimes lurk I in a gossip’s bowl.
One who runs from house to house, tattling and telling news; an idle tattler.
gossiping: Prating; chatting; running from place to place and tattling.
A prating; a running about to collect tales and tattle.
gossipred: Compaternity; spiritual affinity, for which a juror might be challenged.
got: of get. The old preterit gat, pronounced got, is nearly obsolete.
goth: One of an ancient and distinguished tribe or nation, which inhabited Scandinavia, now Sweden and Norway, whose language is now retained in those countries, and a large portion of it is found in English.
One rude or uncivilized; a barbarian.
gothamist: A person deficient in wisdom, so called from Gotham in Nottinghamshire, noted for some pleasant blunders.
gothic: Pertaining to the Goths; as Gothic customs; Gothic architecture; Gothic barbarity.
gothicism: Conformity to the Gothic style of building.
gothicize: To make Gothic; to bring back to barbarism.
gouge: A round hollow chisel, used to cut holes, channels or grooves in wood or stone.
gouj. To scoop out with a gouge.
To force out the eye of a person with the thumb or finger; a barbarous practice.
gouland: A plant or flower. Goulard’s Extract, so called from the inventor, a saturated solution of the subacetate of lead, used as a remedy for inflammation.
gourd: A plant and its fruit, of the genus Cucurbita. There are several species, as the bottle-gourd, the shell-gourd or calabash, the warted gourdThe shell is sometimes used for a piggin or for a bottle.
gourdiness: A swelling on a horse’s leg after a journey.
gourd-tree: A tree, the Crescentia, found in the W. Indies.
gout: The arthritis, a painful disease of the small joints, but sometimes affecting the stomach. It is often periodical or intermitting.
goutiness: The state of being subject to the gout; gouty affections.
gouty: Diseased with the gout, or subject to the gout; as a gouty person; a gouty joint; a gouty constitution.
Pertaining to the gout; as gouty matter.
govern: To direct and control, as the actions or conduct of men, either by established laws or by arbitrary will; to regulate by authority; to keep within the limits prescribed by law or sovereign will. Thus in free states, men are governed by the constitution and laws; in despotic states, men are governed by the edicts or commands of a monarch. Every man should govern well his own family.
To regulate; to influence; to direct. This is the chief point by which he is to govern all his counsels and actions.
To control; to restrain; to keep in due subjection; as, to govern the passions or temper.
To direct; to steer; to regulate the course or motion of a ship. The helm or the helmsman governs the ship.
In grammar, to require to be in a particular case; as, a verb transitive governs a word in the accusative case; or to require a particular case; as, a verb governs the accusative case.
governable: That may be governerned, or subjected to authority; controllable; manageable; obedient; submissive to law or rule.
governance: Government; exercise of authority; direction; control; management, either of a public officer, or of a private guardian or tutor.
governant: A lady who has the care and management of young females; a governess.
governed: Directed; regulated by authority; controlled; managed; influenced; restrained.
governess: A female invested with authority to control and direct; a tutoress; an instructress; a woman who has the care of instructing and directing young ladies.
governing: Directing; controlling; regulating by laws or edicts; managing; influencing; restraining.
Holding the superiority; prevalent; as a governing wind; a governing party na state.
Directing; controlling; as a governing motive.
government: Direction; regulation. These precepts will serve for the government of our conduct.
Control; restraint. Men are apt to neglect the government of their temper and passions.

The exercise of authority; direction and restraint exercised over the actions of men in communities, societies or states; the administration of public § affairs, according to established constitution, laws and usages, or by arbitrary edicts. Prussia rose to importance under the government of Frederick II .

The exercise of authority by a parent or householder. Children are often ruined by a neglect of government in parents. Let family government be like that of our heavenly Father, mild, gentle and affectionate.

The system of polity in a state; that form of fundamental rules and principles by which a nation or state is governed, or by which individual members of a body politic are to regulate their social actions; a constitution, either written or unwritten, by which the rights and duties of citizens and public officers are prescribed and defined; as a monarchial government, or a republican government. Thirteen governments thus founded on the natural authority of the people alone, without the pretence of miracle or mystery, are a great point gained in favor of the rights of mankind.
governmental: Pertaining to government; made by government.
governor: He that governs, rules or directs; one invested with supreme authority. The Creator is the rightful governor of all his creatures.

One who is invested with supreme authority to administer or enforce the laws; the supreme executive magistrate of a state, commmunity, corporation or post. Thus, in America, each state has its governor; Canada has its governor.
A tutor; one who has the care of a young man; one who instructs him and forms his manners.
A pilot; one who steers a ship. James 3:4.
One possessing delegated authority. Joseph was governor over the land of Egypt. Obadiah was governor over Ahab’s house. Damascus had a governor under Aretas the king.
gowan: A plant, a species of Bellis or daisy.
gown: A long loose upper garment or robe, worn by professional men, as divines, lawyers, students, who are called men of the gown or gownmen. It is made of any kind of cloth worn over ordinary clothes, and hangs down to the ankles or nearly so.
The dress of peace, or the civil magistracy; cedant arma togoe. He Mars deposed, and arms to gowns made yield.
gownman: One whose professional habit is a gown. The gownman learn’ed.
One devoted to the arts of peace.
grab: A vessel used on the Malabar coast, having two or three masts.
grabble: To grope; to feel with the hands.
To lie prostrate on the belly; to sprawl.
grace: Favor; good will; kindness; disposition to oblige another; as a grant made as an act of grace. Or each, or all, may win a lady’s grace.
Appropriately, the free unmerited love and favor of God, the spring and source of all the benefits men receive from him. And if by grace, then it is no more of works. Romans 11:6.
Favorable influence of God; divine influence or the influence of the spirit, in renewing the heart and restraining from sin. My grace is sufficient for thee. 2 Corinthians 12:9.
The application of Christ’s righteousness to the sinner. Where sin abounded, grace did much more abound. Romans 5:20.
A state of reconciliation to God. Romans 5:2.
grace-cup: The cup or health drank after grace.
graceful: Beautiful with dignity; elegant; agreeable in appearance, with an expression of dignity or elevation of mind or manner; used particularly of motion, looks and speech; as a graceful walk; a graceful deportment; a graceful speaker; a graceful air. High o’er the rest in arms the graceful Turnus rode.
gracefully: With a pleasing dignity; elegantly; with a natural ease and propriety; as, to walk or speak gracefully.
gracefulness: Elegance of manner or deportment; beauty with dignity in manner, motion or countenance. Gracefulness consists in the natural ease and propriety of an action, accompanied with a countenance expressive of dignity or elevation of mind. Happy is the man who can add the gracefulness of ease to the dignity of merit.
graceless: Void of grace; corrupt; depraved; unregenerate; unsanctified.
gracious: Favorable; kind; friendly; as, the envoy met with a gracious reception.
Favorable; kind; benevolent; merciful; disposed to forgive offenses and impart unmerited blessings. Thou are a God ready to pardon, gracious and merciful. Nehemiah 9:17.
Favorable; expressing kindness and favor. All bore him witness, and wondered at the gracious words which proceeded from his mouth. Luke 4:22.
Proceeding from divine grace; as a person in a gracious state.
Acceptable; favored. He made us gracious before the kings of Persia. Esdras 8:80.
graciously: Kindly; favorably; in a friendly manner; with kind condescension. His testimony he graciously confirmed.
graciousness: Possession of graces or good qualities.
grackle: A genus of birds, the Gracula, of which the crow-blackbird is a species.
gradation: A series of ascending steps or degrees, or a proceeding step by step; hence, progress from one degree or state to another; a regular advance from step to step. We observe a gradation in the progress of society from a rude to civilized life. Men may arrive by several gradations to the most horrid impiety.
A degree in any order or series; we observe a gradation in the scale of being, from brute to mane, from man to angels.
Order; series; regular process by degrees or steps; as a gradation in argument or description.
gradatory: Steps from the cloisters into the church.
grade: A degree or rank in order or dignity, civil, military or ecclesiastical. While questions, periods, and grades and privileges are never once formally discussed.
A step or degree in any ascending series; as crimes of every grade. When we come to examine the intermediate grades.
gradient: Moving by steps; walking; as gradient automata.
gradual: Proceeding by steps or degrees; advancing step by step; passing from one step to another; regular and slow; as a gradual increase of knowledge; a gradual increase of light in the morning is favorable to the eyes.
Proceeding by degrees in a descending line or progress; as a gradual decline.
gradaul: A grail; an ancient book of hymns and prayers.
gradually: By degrees; step by step; regularly; slowly. At evening the light vanishes gradually.
In degree. Human reason doth not only gradually, but specifically differ from the fantastic reason of brutes.
graduate: To honor with a degree or diploma, in a college or university; to confer a degree on; as, to graduate a master of arts.
To mark with degrees, regular intervals, or division; as, to graduate a thermometer.
To form shades or nice differences.
To raise to a higher place in the scale of metals.
To advance by degrees; to improve. Dyers advance and graduate their colors with salts.
graduated: Honored with a degree or diploma from some learned society or college.
Marked with degrees or regular intervals; tempered.
graduating: Honoring with a degree; marking with degrees.
graduation: Regular progression by succession of degrees.
The act of conferring or receiving academical degrees.
The act of marking with degrees.
The process of bringing a liquid to a certain consistence by evaporation.
graduator: An instrument for dividing any line, right or curve, into equal parts.
graft: A small shoot or cion of a tree, inserted in another tree as the stock which is to support and nourish it. These unite and become one tree, but the graft determines the kind of fruit.
grafter: One who inserts cions on foreign stocks, or propagates fruit by ingrafting.
grafting: Inserting cions on different stocks. Note. The true original orthography of this word is graff; but graft has superseded the original word, as it has in the compound ingraft.
grail: A book of offices in the Romish church.
grain: Any small hard mass; as a grain of sand or gravel. Hence,

A single seed or hard seed of a plant, particularly of those kinds whose seeds are used for food of man or beast. This is usually inclosed in a proper shell or covered with a husk, and contains the embryo of a new plant. Hence,
Grain, without a definitive, signifies corn in general, or the fruit of certain plants which constitutes the chief food of man and beast, as wheat, rye, barley, oats and maiz.
A small weight, or the smallest weight ordinarily used, being the twentieth part of the scruple in apothecaries’ weight, and the twenty fourth of a pennyweight troy.
grainer: A lixivium obtained by infusing pigeon’s dung in water; used by tanners to give flexibility to skins.
graining: A fish of the dace kind.
grains: The husks or remains of malt after brewing, or of any grain after distillation. Grains of paradise, an Indian spice, the seeds of a species of Amomum.
grainy: Full of grains or corn; full of kernels.
grallic: Stilted; an epithet given to an order of fowls having long legs, naked above the knees, which fit them for wading in water.
gram: In the new system of French weights, the unity of weights. It is the weight of a quantity of distilled water equal to a cubic centimeter, or 18 grains French, or du poids de marc, equal to 15.444 grains troy.
gramercy: for Fr. grand-merci, is not in use. It formerly was used to express obligation.
gramineal: Grassy; like or pertaining to grass. Gramineous plants are those which have simple leaves, a jointed stem, a husky calyx, termed glume, and a single seed. This description however includes several sorts of corn, as well as grass.
gramineous: Grassy; like or pertaining to grass. Gramineous plants are those which have simple leaves, a jointed stem, a husky calyx, termed glume, and a single seed. This description however includes several sorts of corn, as well as grass.
graminivorous: Feeding or subsisting on grass. The ox and all the bovine genus of quadrupeds are graminivorous animals; so also the horse or equine genus.
grammar: In practice, the art of speaking or writing a language with propriety or correctness, according to established usage. As a science, grammar treats of the natural connection between ideas and words, and develops the principles which are common to all languages.

A system of general principles and of particular rules for speaking or writing a language; or digested compilation of customary forms of speech in a nation; also, a book containing such principles and rules.
Propriety of speech. To write grammar, we must write according to the practice of good writers and speakers.
To discourse according to the rules of grammar.
Belonging to or contained in grammar; as a grammar rule.
grammar-school: A school in which the learned languages are taught. By learned languages, we usually mean the Latin and Greek; but others may be included.
grammarian: One versed in grammar, or the construction of languages; a philologist.
grammatical: Belonging to grammar; as a grammatical rule.
According to the rules of grammar. We say, a sentence is not grammatical; the construction is not grammatical.
grammatically: According to the principles and rules of grammar; as, to write or speak grammatically.
