grammaticaster: A low grammarian; a pretender to a knowledge of grammar; a pedant.
grammatist: A pretender to a knowledge of grammar.
grampus: A fish of the cetaceous order, and genus Delphinus. This fish grows to the length of twenty five feet, and is remarkably thick in proportion to its length. The nose is flat and turns up at the end. It has 30 teeth in each jaw. The spout-hole is on the top of the neck. The color of the back is black; the belly is of a snowy whiteness; and on each shoulder is a large white spot. This fist is remarkably voracious.
granadilla: A plant; the fruit of the Passiflora quadrangulata.
granary: A store house or repository of grain after it is thrashed; a corn- house.
grand: Great; but mostly in a figurative sense; illustrious; high in power or dignity; as a grand lord.
Great; splendid; magnificent; as a grand design; a grand parade; a grand view or prospect.
Great; principal; chief; as Satan our grand foe.

Noble; sublime; lofty; conceived or expressed with great dignity; as a grand conception. In general, we apply the epithet grand to that which is great and elevated, or which elevates and expands our ideas. The ocean, the sky, a lofty tower are grand objects. But to constitute a thing grand, it seems necessary that it should be distinguished by some degree of beauty.
Old; more advanced; as in grandfather, grandmother, that is, old- father; and to correspond with this relation, we use grandson, granddaughter.
grandchild: A son’s or daughter’s child; a child in the second degree of descent.
granddaughter: The daughter of a son or daughter.
grandee: A nobleman; a man of elevated rank or station. In Spain, a nobleman of the first rank, who has the king’s leave to be covered in his presence.
grandeeship: The rank or estate of a grandee.
grandeur: In a general sense, greatness; that quality or combination of qualities in an object, which elevates or expands the mind, and excites pleasurable emotions in him who views or contemplates it. Thus the extent and uniformity of surface in the ocean constitute grandeur; as do the extent, the elevation, and the concave appearance or vault of the sky. So we speak of the grandeur of a large and well proportioned edifice, of an extensive range of lofty mountains, of a large cataract, of a pyramid. etc.
Splendor of appearance; state; magnificence; as the grandeur of a court, of a procession. etc.
Elevation of thought, sentiment or expression. We speak of the grandeur of conceptions, and of style or diction.
Elevation of mien or air and deportment.
grandfather: A father’s or mother’s father; the next degree above the father or mother in lineal ascent.
grandjuror: One of a grand jury. In Connecticut, a peace-officer.
grand jury: A jury whose duty is to examine into the grounds of accusation against offenders, and if they see just cause, then to find bills of indictment against them to be presented to the court.
grandly: In a lofty manner; splendidly; sublimely.
grandmother: The mother of one’s father or mother.
grandsire: In poetry and rhetoric, any ancestor.
grandson: The son of a son or daughter.
grange: A farm, with the buildings, stables
granilite: Indeterminate granit; granit that contains more than three constituent parts.
granit: In mineralogy, an aggregate stone or rock, composed of crystalline grains of quartz, feldspar and mica, or at least of two of these minerals, united without a cement, or confusedly crystallized. The grains very in size from that of a pin’s head, to a mass of two or three feet; but usually the largest size is that of a nut. The color of granit is greatly diversified by the different colors and proportions of the component parts, and in general these stones are very hard.
granite: In mineralogy, an aggregate stone or rock, composed of crystalline grains of quartz, feldspar and mica, or at least of two of these minerals, united without a cement, or confusedly crystallized. The grains very in size from that of a pin’s head, to a mass of two or three feet; but usually the largest size is that of a nut. The color of granit is greatly diversified by the different colors and proportions of the component parts, and in general these stones are very hard.
granitel: A binary aggregate of minerals; a granitic compound containing two constituent parts, as quartz and feldspar, or quartz and shorl or hornblend. Italian workmen give this name to a variety of gray granit consisting of small grains.
granitic: Pertaining to granit; like granit; having the nature of granitic; as granitic texture.

Consisting of granit; as granitic mountains. Granitic aggregates, in mineralogy, granular compounds of two or more simple minerals, in which only one of the essential ingredients of granit is present; as quartz and hornblend, feldspar and shorlSimilar compounds occur, in which none of the ingredients of granit are present.
granitin: A granitic aggregate of three species of minerals, some of which differ from the species which compose granit; as quartz, feldspar, and jade or shorl.
granivorous: Eating grain; feeding or subsisting on seeds; as granivorous birds. Grannam, for grandam, a grandmother.
grant: The act of granting; a bestowing or conferring.
To admit as true what is not proved; to allow; to yield; to concede. We take that for granted which is supposed to be true. Grant that the fates have firmed, by their decree-- .

To give; to bestow or confer on without compensation, particularly in answer to prayer or request. Thou hast granted me life and favor. Job 10:12. God granted him that which he requested. 7 Chronicles 4:10.

To transfer the title of a thing to another, for a good or valuable consideration; to convey by deed or writing. The legislature have granted all the new land. Grant me the place of this threshing floor. 7 Chronicles 21:22.
grantable: That may be granted or conveyed.
granted: Admitted as true; conceded; yielded; bestowed; conveyed.
grantee: The person to whom a conveyance is made.
grantor: The person who grants; one who conveys lands, rents
granular: Consisting of grains; as a granular substance.
Resembling grains; as a stone of granular appearance.
granulate: To form into grains or small masses; as, to granulate powder or sugar.
To raise into small asperities; to make rough on the surface.
To collect or be formed into grains; as cane-juice granulates into sugar; melted metals granulate when poured into water.
granulation: The act of forming into grains; as the granulation of powder and sugar. In chimistry, the granulation of metallic substances is performed by pouring the melted substances slowly into water, which is, at the same time, agitated with a broom.
granule: A little grain; a small particle.
granulous: Full of grains; abounding with granular substances.
grape: Properly, a cluster of the fruit of the vine; but with us, a single berry of the vine; the fruit from which wine is made by expression and fermentation.
In the manege, grapes signifies mangy tumors on the legs of a horse.
grape-hyacinth: A plant or flower, a species of Hyacinthus.
grapeless: Wanting the strength and flavor of the grape.
grapeshot: A cluster of small shot, confined in a canvas bag, forming a kind of cylinder, whose diameter is equal to that of the ball adapted to the cannon.
grapestone: The stone or seed of the grape.
graphic: Pertaining to the art of writing or delineating.
graphical: Pertaining to the art of writing or delineating.
graphically: With good delineation; in a picturesque manner.
graphite: Carburet of iron, a substance used for pencils, and very improperly called black-lead.
grapholite: A species of slate proper for writing on.
graphometer: A mathematical instrument, called also a semicircle, whose use is to observe any angle whose vertex is at the center of the instrument in any plane, and to find how many degrees it contains. Pala
graphometrical: Pertaining to or ascertained by a graphometer.
grapnel: Asmall anchor fitted with four or five flukes or claws, used to hold boats or small vessels.
A grappling iron, used to seize and hold one ship to another in engagements. This is called a fire grapling.
grapling: Asmall anchor fitted with four or five flukes or claws, used to hold boats or small vessels.
A grappling iron, used to seize and hold one ship to another in engagements. This is called a fire grapling.
grapple: To seize; to lay fast hold on, either with the hands or with hooks. We say, a man grapples his antagonist, or a ship grapples another ship.
To fasten; to fix, as the mind or heart.
To seize; to contend in close fight, as wrestlers. To grapple with, to contend with, to struggle with successfully.
A seizing; close hug in contest; the wrestler’s hold.
grapplement: A grappling; close fight or embrace.
grapy: Like grapes; full of clusters of grapes.
grasp: To seize and hold by clasping or embracing with the fingers or arms. We say, to grasp with the hand, or with the arms.
To catch; to seize; to lay hold of; to take possession of. Kings often grasp more than they can hold.
grasped: Seized with the hands or arms, embraced; held; possessed.
grasper: One who grasps or seizes; one who catches at; one who holds.
grass: In common usage, herbage; the plants which constitute the food of cattle and other beasts.

In botany, a plant having simple leaves, a stem generally jointed and tubular, a husky calyx, called glume, and the seed single. This definition includes wheat, rye, oats, barley, and excludes clover and some other plants which are commonly called by the name of grass. The grasses form a numerous family of plants. Grass of Parnassus, a plant, the Parnassia.
grass-green: Dark green, like the color of grass.
grasshopper: An animal that lives among grass, a species of Gryllus.
grassiness: The state of abounding with grass; a grassy state.
grassplot: A plat or level spot covered with grass.
grasspoly: A plant, a species of Lythrum or willow-wort.
grassvetch: A plant of the genus Lathyrus.
grassy: Covered with grass; abounding with grass.
grate: A work or frame, composed of parallel or cross bars, with interstices; a kind of lattice-work, such as is used in the windows of prisons and cloisters.
An instrument or frame of iron bars for holding coals, used as fuel, in houses, stores, shops.
To furnish with grates; to make fast with cross bars.
To rub, as a body with a rough surface against another body; to rub one thing against another, so as to produce a harsh sound; as, to grate the teeth.
To wear away in small particles, by rubbing with any thing rough or indented; as, to grate a nutmeg.
grated: Rubbed harshly; worn off by rubbing.
Furnished with a grate; as grated windows.
grateful: Having a due sense of benefits; kindly disposed towards one from whom a favor has been received; willing to acknowledge and repay benefits; as a grateful heart.
Agreeable; pleasing; acceptable; gratifying; as a grateful present; a grateful offering.
Pleasing to the taste; delicious; affording pleasure; as food or drink grateful offering. Now golden fruits on loaded branches shine, And grateful clusters swell with floods of wine.
gratefully: With a due sense of benefits or favors; in a manner that disposes to kindness, in return for favors. The gift was gratefully received.
In a pleasing manner. Study continually furnishes something new, which may strike the imagination gratefully.
gratefulness: The quality of being grateful; gratitude.
The quality of being agreeable or pleasant to the mind or to the taste.
grater: An instrument or utensil with a rough indented surface, for rubbing off small particles of a body; as a grater for nutmegs.
gratification: The act of pleasing, either the mind, the taste or the appetite. We speak of the gratification of the taste or the palate, of the appetites, of the senses, of the desires, of the mind, soul or heart.
That which affords pleasure; satisfaction; delight. It is not easy to renounce gratifications to which we are accustomed.
gratify: To please; to give pleasure to; to indulge; as, to gratify the taste, the appetite, the senses, the desires, the mind.
To delight; to please; to humor; to soothe; to satisfy; to indulge to satisfaction. For who would die to gratify a foe?
grating: Fretting; irritating; harsh; as grating sounds, or a grating reflection.
A partition of bars; an open cover for the hatches of a ship, resembling lattice-work.
gratings: A partition of bars; an open cover for the hatches of a ship, resembling lattice-work.
gratingly: Harshly; offensively; in a manner to irritate.
gratis: For nothing; freely; without recompense; as, to give a thing gratis; to perform service gratis.
gratitude: An emotion of the heart, excited by a favor or benefit received; a sentiment of kindness or good will towards a benefactor; thankfulness. Gratitude is an agreeable emotion, consisting in or accompanied with good will to a benefactor, and a disposition to make a suitable return of benefits or services, or when no return can be made, with a desire to see the benefactor prosperous and happy. Gratitude is a virtue of the highest excellence, as it implies a feeling and generous heart, and a proper sense of duty. The love of God is the sublimest gratitude.
gratuitous: Free; voluntary; not required by justice; granted without claim or merit. We mistake the gratuitous blessings of heaven for the fruits of our own industry.
Asserted or taken without proof; as a gratuitous argument or affirmation.
gratuitously: Freely; voluntarily; without claim or merit; without an equivalent or compensation; as labor or services gratuitously bestowed.
Without proof; as a principle gratuitously assumed.
gratuity: A free gift; a present; a donation; that which is given without a compensation or equivalent.
Something given in return for a favor; an acknowledgment.
gratulate: To express joy or pleasure to a person, on account of his success, or the reception of some good; to salute with declarations of joy; to congratulate. To gratulate the gentle princes there.
To wish or express joy to.
To declare joy for; to mention with joy.
gratulating: Addressing with expressions of joy, on account of some good received.
gratulation: An address or expression ofjoy to a person, on account of some good received by him; congratulation. I shall turn my wishes into gratulations.
grave: a final syllable, is a grove.
pret. graved; pp. graven or graved.

To carve or cut letters or figures on stone or other hard substance, with a chisel or edged tool; to engrave. Thou shalt take two onyx-stones and grave on them the names of the children of Israel. Exodus 28:9.
To carve; to form or shape by cutting with a chisel; as, to grave an image. Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven image. Exodus 20:4.
To clean a ship’s bottom by burning off filth, grass or other foreign matter, and paying it over with pitch.
grave-clothes: The clothes or dress in which the dead are interred.
grave-digger: One whose occupation is to dig graves.
grave-stone: A stone laid over a grave, or erected near it, as a monument to preserve the memory of the dead.
graved: Carved; engraved; cleaned, as a ship.
gravel: Small stones or fragments of stone, or very small pebbles, larger than the particles of sand, but often intermixed with them.
In medicine, small calculous concretions in the kidneys and bladder.
To cover with gravel; as, to gravel a walk.
To puzzle; to stop; to embarrass.
graveled: Covered with gravel; stopped; embarrassed; injured by gravel.
graveless: Without a grave or tomb; unburied.
gravelly: Abounding with gravel; consisting of gravel; as a gravelly soil or land.
gravel-walk: A walk or alley covered with gravel, which makes a hard and dry bottom; used in gardens and malls.
gravely: In a grave, solemn manner; soberly; seriously. The queen of learning gravely smiles.
Without gaudiness or show; as, to be dressed gravely.
graveness: Seriousness; solemnity; sobriety of behavior; gravity of manners or discourse.
graver: One who carves or engraves; one whose profession is to cut letters or figures in stone; a sculptor.
An engraving tool; an instrument for graving on hard substances.
graving: Engraving; carving; cutting figures on stone, copper or other hard substance.
gravitate: To tend to the center of a body, or the central point of attraction. Thus a body elevated above the earth tends to fall, that is, it gravitates towards the center of the earth; and the planets are supposed to gravitate towards the sun, or center of the solar system.
gravitating: Tending to the center of a body or system of bodies.
gravitation: The act of tending to the center.

The force by which bodies are pressed or drawn, or by which they tend towards the center of the earth or other center, or the effect of that force. Thus the falling of a body to the earth is ascribed to gravitation.
gravity: In philosophy, that force by which bodies tend or are pressed or drawn towards the center of the earth, or towards some other center, or the effect of that force; in which last sense gravity is synonymous with weight. Gravity is the tendency of great bodies to a center, or the sum or results of all the attractions of all the molecules composing a great body.

Specific gravity, the weight belonging to an equal bulk of every different substance. Thus the exact weight of a cubic inch of gold, compared with that of a cubic inch of gold, compared with that of a cubic inch of water or tin, is called its specific gravity. The specific gravity of bodies is usually ascertained by weighing them in distilled water.
Seriousness; sobriety of manners; solemnity of deportment or character. Great Cato there, for gravity renowned.
Weight; enormity; atrociousness; as the gravity of an injury.
gravy: The fat and other liquid matter that drips from flesh in roasting, or when roasted or baked, or a mixture of that juice with flour.
gray: The legal power of authority of doing justice in cases of complaint; the power of executing the laws and distributing justice. Thus we speak of certain suits or actions, or the cognizance of certain crimes being within the jurisdiction of a court, that is, within the limits of their authority or commission. Inferior courts have jurisdiction of debt and trespass, or of smaller offenses; the supreme courts have jurisdiction of treason, murder, and other high crimes. Jurisdiction is secular or — ecclesiastical.
White, with a mixture of black. These gray and dun colors may be also produced by mixing whites and blacks.
White; hoary; as gray hair. We apply the word to hair that is partially or wholly white.
Dark; of a mixed color; of the color of ashes; as gray eyes; the gray-eyed morn.
gray-headed: Having a gray head or gray hair.
grayhound: A tall fleet dog, used in the chase.
grayish: Somewhat gray; gray in a moderate degree.
grayling: A fish of the genus Salmo, called also umber, a voracious fish, about sixteen or eighteen inches in length, of a more elegant figure than the trout; the back and sides of a silvery gray color. It is found in clear rapid streams in the north of Europe, and is excellent food.
graywacke: A rock somewhat remarkable in its structure and geological relations; a kind of sandstone, composed of grains or fragments of different minerals, chiefly of quartz, feldspar, siliceous slate and argillite. These fragments are sometimes angular, and sometimes their edges and angles are rounded, thus forming nodules or globular masses. The size is very variable, passing from grains to nodules of a foot in diameter. The several ingredients are united by an indurated argillaceous substance, or the interstices between the larger fragments are filled by the same materials which compose the larger parts of the rock, but in grains so comminuted as to resemble a homogeneous cement. The colors are some shade of gray or brown, as bluish gray, reddish brown. etc.
graze: To rub or touch lightly in passing; to brush lightly in passing; as, the bullet grazed the wall or the earth.
To feed or supply cattle with grass; to furnish pasture for; as, the farmer grazes large herds of cattle.
To feed on; to eat from the ground, as growing herbage. The lambs with wolves shall graze the verdant mead.
To tend grazing cattle; as, Jacob grazed Laban’s sheep.
To eat grass; to feed on growing herbage; as, cattle graze on the meadows.
grazed: Touched lightly by a passing body; brushed.
Fed by growing grass; as, cattle are grazed.
Eaten, as growing herbage; as, the fields were grazed.
grazer: One that grazes or feeds on growing herbage.
grazier: gra’zhur. One who feeds cattle with grass, or supplies them with pasture.
grazing: Touching lightly, as a moving body.
Feeding on growing herbage; as grazing cattle.
Supplying pasture; as a grazing farm.
grease: Animal fat in a soft state; oily or unctuous matter of any kind, as tallow, lard; but particularly the fatty matter of land animals, as distinguished from the oily matter of marine animals.
A swelling and gourdiness of a horse’s legs, occasioned by traveling or by standing long in a stable.
greez. To smear, anoint or daub with grease or fat.
To bribe; to corrupt with presents.
greasily: With grease or an appearance of it; grossly.
greasiness: The state of being greasy; oiliness; unctuousness.
greasing: Smearing with fat or oily matter; bribing.
greasy: Like grease or oil; smooth; as a fossil that has a greasy feel .
great: Large in bulk or dimensions; a term of comparison, denoting more magnitude or extension than something else, or beyond what is usual; as a great body; a great house; a great farm.
Being of extended length or breadth; as a great distance; a great lake.
Large in number; as a great many; a great multitude.
Expressing a large, extensive or unusual degree of any thing; as great fear; great love; great strength; great wealth; great power; great influence; great folly; .
Long continued; as a great while.
greatly: In a great degree; much. I will greatly multiply thy sorrow. Genesis 3:16.
Nobly; illustriously. By a high fate, thou greatly didst expire.
Magnanimously; generously; bravely. He greatly scorned to turn his back on his foe. He greatly spurned the offered boon.
greatness: Largeness of bulk, dimensions, number or quantity; as the greatness of a mountain, of an edifice, of a multitude, or of a sum of money. With reference to solid bodies, however, we more generally use bulk, size, extent or magnitude than greatness; as the bulk or size of the body; the extent of the ocean; the magnitude of the sun or of the earth.
Large amount; extent; as the greatness of a reward.
High degree; as the greatness of virtue or vice.
High rank or place; elevation; dignity; distinction; eminence; power; command.
Swelling pride; affected state. It is not of pride or greatness that he cometh not aboard your ships.
greaves: greevz. Armor for the legs; a sort of boots. 1 Samuel 17:6.
grebe: A fowl of the genus Colymbus and order of ansers, of several species; as the tippet-grebe, the horned grebe, the eared grebe or dob-chick.
grecian: A native of Greece. Also, a Jew who understood Greek. Acts 6:1.
One well versed in the Greek language.
grecism: An idiom of the Greek language. assist the master or commander. In a merchant ship, the mate, in the absence of the master, takes command of the ship. Large ships have a first, second, and third mate. MATE. In general, mate, in compound words, denotes an assistant, and ranks next in subordination to the principal; as master’s mate; surgeon’s mateMATE.
greet: To address with expressions of kind wishes; to salute in kindness and respect. My lord, the Mayor if London comes to greet you.
To address at meeting; to address in any manner.
To pay compliments at a distance; to send kind wishes to. Colossians 4:14; 2 Timothy 4:21.
To meet and address with kindness; or to express kind wishes accompanied with an embrace. 7 Thessalonians 5:26.
greeting: Addressing with kind wishes or expressions of joy; complimenting; congratulating; saluting.
Expression of kindness or joy; salutation at meeting; compliment addressed from one absent.
greeze: A step, or flight of steps.
gregarian: Belonging to the herd or common sort.
gregarious: Having the habit of assembling or living in a flock or herd; not habitually solitary or living alone. Cattle and sheep are gregarious animals. Many species of birds are gregarious. Rapacious animals are generally not gregarious.
gregariously: In a flock or herd; in a company.
gregariousness: The state or quality of living in flocks or herds.
gregorian: Denoting what belongs to Gregory. The Gregorian calendar, is one which shows the new and full moon, with the time of Easter, and the movable feasts depending thereon, by means of epacts. The Gregorian year, is the present year, as reformed by pope Gregory XIll, in 1582; consisting of 365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes, seconds, with an additional day every fourth year.
gremial: Belonging to the lap or bosom.
grenade: In the art of war, a hollow ball or shell of iron or other metal, about two inches and a half in diameter, to be filled with powder which is to be fired by means of a fusee, and thrown by hand among enemies. This, bursting into many pieces, does great injury, and is particularly useful in annoying an enemy in trenches and other lodgments.
grenadier: A foot soldier, wearing a high cap. Grenadiers are usually tall, active soldiers, distinguished from others chiefly by their dress and arms; a company of them is usually attached to each battalion.
A fowl found in Angola, in Africa.
grenatite: Staurotide or staurolite, a mineral of a dark reddish brown. It occurs imbedded in mica slate, and in talc, and is infusible by the blowpipe. It is called also prismatic garnet.
greyhound: A tall fleet dog, kept for the chase.
griddle: A pan, broad and shallow, for baking cakes.
gride: To grate, or to cut with a grating sound; to cut; to penetrate or pierce harshly; as the griding sword. That through his thigh the mortal steel did gride.
gridelin: A color mixed of white and red, or a gray violet.
gridiron: A grated utensil for broiling flesh and fish over coals.
grief: The pain of mind produced by loss, misfortune, injury or evils of any kind; sorrow; regret. We experience grief when we lose a friend, when we incur loss, when we consider ourselves injured, and by sympathy, we feel grief at the misfortunes of others.

The pain of mind occasioned by our own misconduct; sorrow or regret that we have done wrong; pain accompanying repentance. We feel grief when we have offended or injured a friend, and the consciousness of having offended the Supreme Being, fills the penitent heart with the most poignant grief.
Cause of sorrow; that which afflicts. Who were a grief of mind to Isaac and Rebekah. Genesis 26:35. A foolish son is a grief to his father. Proverbs 17:25.
grievance: That which causes grief or uneasiness; that which burdens, oppresses or injures, implying a sense of wrong done, or a continued injury, and therefore applied only to the effects of human conduct; never to providential evils. The oppressed subject has the right to petition for a redress of grievances.
grieve: To give pain of mind to; to afflict; to wound the feelings. Nothing grieves a parent like the conduct of a profligate child.
To afflict; to inflict pain on. For he doth not afflict willingly, nor grieve the children of men. Lamentations 3:33.
To make sorrowful; to excite regret in.
To offend; to displease; to provoke. Grieve not the holy Spirit of God. Ephesians 4:30.

To feel pain of mind or heart; to be in pain on account of an evil; to sorrow; to mourn. We grieve at the loss of friends or property. We grieve at the misfortunes of others. We grieve for our own misfortunes, follies and vices, as well as for those of our children. It is followed by at or for.
grievous: Heavy; oppressive; burdensome; as a grievous load of taxes.
Afflictive; painful; hard to be borne. Correction is grievous to him that forsaketh the way. Proverbs 15:10.
Causing grief or sorrow. The thing was very grievous in Abraham’s sight, because of his son. Genesis 21:11.
Distressing. The famine was very grievous in the land. Genesis 12:10.
Great; atrocious. Because their sin is very grievous. Genesis 18:20.
grievously: With pain; painfully; with great pain or distress; as, to be grievously afflicted.
With discontent, ill will or grief.
Calamitously; miserably; greatly; with great uneasiness, distress or grief.
Atrociously; as, to sin or offend grievously.
grievousness: Oppressiveness; weight that gives pain or distress; as the grievousness of a burden.
Pain; affliction; calamity; distress; as the grievousness of sickness, war or famine.
Greatness; enormity; atrociousness; as the grievousness of sin or offenses.
griffon: In the natural history of the ancients, an imaginary animal said to be generated between the lion and eagle. It is represented with four legs, wings and a beak, the upper part resembling an eagle, and the lower part a lion. This animal was supposed to watch over mines of gold and hidden treasures, and was consecrated to the sun. The figure of the griffon is seen on ancient medals, and is still borne in coat-armor. It is also an ornament of Greek architecture.
grig: A small eel; the sand ell.
grim: Fierce; ferocious; impressing terror; frightful; horrible; as a grim look; a grim face; grim war.
grimace: A distortion of the countenance, from habit, affectation or insolence.
grimalkin: The name of an old cat.
grime: Foul matter; dirt; sullying blackness, deeply insinuated.
To sully or soil deeply; to dirt.
grimly: Having a hideous or stern look.
Fiercely; ferociously; with a look of fury or ferocity.
grin: To set the teeth together and open the lips, or to open the mouth and withdraw the lips from the teeth, so as to show them, as in laughter or scorn. Fools grin on fools.
To fix the teeth, as in anguish.
The act of closing the teeth and showing them, or of withdrawing the lips and showing the teeth.
To express by grinning. He grinned horribly a ghastly smile.&
grind: To break and reduce to fine particles or powder by friction; to comminute by attrition; to triturate. Take the millstones and grind meal. Isaiah 47:2. We say, to grind meal, but this is an elliptical phrase. The true phrase is, to grind corn to meal.
To break and reduce to small pieces by the teeth.
To sharpen by rubbing or friction; to wear off the substance of a metallic instrument, and reduce it to a sharp edge by the friction of a stone; as, to grind an ax or scythe.
To make smooth; to polish by friction; as, to grind glass.
grinder: One that grinds, or moves a mill.
A tooth that grinds or chews food; a double tooth; a jaw- tooth.
grinding: Reducing to powder by friction; triturating; levitating; chewing.
Making sharp; making smooth or polishing by friction.
grindstone: A sandstone used for grinding or sharpening tools. Stone, used by old writers, is obsolete.
grinning: Closing the teeth and showing them, as in laughter; a showing of the teeth.
gripe: To seize; to grasp; to catch with the hand, and to clasp closely with the fingers.
To hold fast; to hold with the fingers closely pressed.
To seize and hold fast in the arms; to embrace closely.
To close the fingers; to clutch.
To pinch; to press; to compress.
griper: One who gripes; an oppressor; an extortioner.
griping: Grasping; seizing; holding fast; pinching; oppressing; distressing the bowels.
A pinching or grasp; a distressing pain of the bowels; colic.
In seamen’s language, the inclination of a ship to run to the windward of her course.
gripingly: With a pain in the bowels.
grise: A step, or scale of steps.
grisette: griset’. A tradesman’s wife or daughter.
grisly: s as z. Frightful; horrible; terrible; as grisly locks; a grisly countenance; a grisly face; a grisly specter; a grisly bear.
grisons: Inhabitants of the eastern Swiss alps.
grist: Properly, that which is ground; hence, corn ground; but in common usage, it signifies corn for grinding, or that which is ground at one time; as much grain as is carried to the mill at one time or the meal it produces. Get grist to the mill to have plenty in store.
Profit; gain; in the phrase, it brings grist to the mill: .
gristle: gris’l. A cartilage; a smooth, solid, elastic substance in animal bodies, chiefly in those parts where a small easy motion is required, as in the nose, ear, larynx, trachea and sternum. It covers the ends of all bones which are united by movable articulations.
gristly: Consisting of gristle; like gristle; cartilaginous; as the gristly rays of fins connected by membranes.
grit: Oats hulled, or coarsely ground; written also groats.
Sand or gravel; rough hard particles.
Sandstone; stone composed of particles of sand agglutinated.
grittiness: The quality of containing grit or consisting of grit, sand or small hard, rough particles of stone.
gritty: Containing sand or grit; consisting of grit; full of hard particles; sandy.
grizzle: Gray; a gray color; a mixture of white and black.
grizzled: Gray; of a mixed color. Genesis 31:10, 12.
groan: To breathe with a deep murmuring sound; to utter a mournful voice, as in pain or sorrow. For we that are in this tabernacle, do groan, being burdened. Corinthians 5:4.
To sigh; to be oppressed or afflicted; or to complain of oppression. A nation groans under the weight of taxes.
A deep mournful sound, uttered in pain, sorrow or anguish.
Any low, rumbling sound; as the groans of roaring wind.
groaning: The act of groaning; lamentation; complaint; a deep sound uttered in pain or sorrow. I have heard the groaning of the children of Israel. Exodus 6:5.
In hunting, the cry or noise of the buck.
groat: An English money of account, equal to four pence.
A proverbial name for a small sum.
groats: Oats that have the hulls taken off.
grocer: A trader who deals in tea, sugar, spices, coffee, liquors, fruits
grocery: The commodities sold by grocers; usually in the plural.
grog: A mixture of spirit and water not sweetened.
grog-blossom: A rum bud; a redness on the nose or face of men who drink ardent spirits to excess; a deformity that marks the beastly vice of intemperance.
groggy: A groggy horse is one that bears wholly on his heels in trotting.
grogram: A kind of stuff made of silk and mohair.
grogran: A kind of stuff made of silk and mohair.
groin: The depressed part of the human body between the belly and the thigh.
Among builders, the angular curve made by the intersection of two semi-cylinders or arches.
The snout or nose of a swine.
gromwell: A plant of the genus Lithospermum. The German gromwell is the Stellera.
gromil: A plant of the genus Lithospermum. The German gromwell is the Stellera.
gromet: Among seamen, a ring formed of a strand of rope laid in three times round; used to fasten the upper edge of a sail to its stay.
grommet: Among seamen, a ring formed of a strand of rope laid in three times round; used to fasten the upper edge of a sail to its stay.
groom: A boy or young man; a waiter; a servant.
Aman or boy who has the charge of horses; one who takes care of horses or the stable.
In England, an officer of the king’s household; as the groom of the chamber; groom of the stole or wardrobe.
Groom for goom, in bridegroom, is a palpable mistake.
groove: A furrow, channel, or long hollow cut by a tool. Among joiners, a channel in the edge of a molding, style or rail.
Among miners, a shaft or pit sunk into the earth.
To cut a channel with an edged tool; to furrow.
grope: To feel along; to search or attempt to find in the dark, or as a blind person, by feeling. We grope for the wall like the blind. Isaiah 59:10. The dying believer leaves the weeping children of mortality to grope a little longer among the miseries and sensualities of a worldly life.
To seek blindly in intellectual darkness, without a certain guide or means of knowledge.
To search by feeling in the dark. We groped our way at midnight. But Strephon, cautious, never meant . The bottom of the pan to grope.
groper: One who gropes; one who feels his way in the dark, or searches by feeling.
groping: Feeling for something in darkness; searching by feeling.
gross: Thick; bulky; particularly applied to animals; fat; corpulent; as a gross man; a gross body.
Coarse; rude; rough; not delicate; as gross sculpture.
Coarse, in a figurative sense; rough; mean; particularly, vulgar; obscene; indelicate; as gross language; gross jests.
Thick; large; opposed to fine; as wood or stone of a gross grain.
grossbeak: A fowl of the genus Loxia, of several species. The bill is convex above and very thick at the base, from which circumstance it takes its name.
grossly: In bulky or large parts; coarsely. This matter is grossly pulverized.
Greatly; palpably; enormously; as, this affair has been grossly misrepresented.
Coarsely; without refinement or delicacy; as language grossly vulgar.
grossness: Thickness; bulkiness; corpulence; fatness; applied to animal bodies.
Thickness; spissitude; density; as the grossness of vapors.
Coarseness; rudeness; want of refinement or delicacy; vulgarity; as the grossness of language; the grossness of wit. Abhor the swinish grossness that delights to wound the ear of delicacy.
Greatness; enormity; as the grossness of vice.
grossular: Pertaining to or resembling a gooseberry; as grossular garnet.
A rare mineral of the garnet kind, so named from its green color.
grot: A large cave or den; a subterraneous cavern, and primarily a natural cave or rent in the earth, or such as is formed by a current of water, or an earthquake.
grotto: A large cave or den; a subterraneous cavern, and primarily a natural cave or rent in the earth, or such as is formed by a current of water, or an earthquake.
grotesque: Wildly formed; whimsical; extravagant; of irregular forms and proportions; ludicrous; antic; resembling the figure found in the subterraneous apartments in the ancient ruins at Rome; applied to pieces of sculpture and painting, and to natural scenery; as grotesque painting; grotesque design.
grotesk: Wildly formed; whimsical; extravagant; of irregular forms and proportions; ludicrous; antic; resembling the figure found in the subterraneous apartments in the ancient ruins at Rome; applied to pieces of sculpture and painting, and to natural scenery; as grotesque painting; grotesque design.
ground: The surface of land or upper part of the earth, without reference to the materials which compose it. We apply ground to soil, sand or gravel indifferently, but never apply it to the whole mass of the earth or globe, nor to any portion of it when removed. We never say a shovel full or a load of ground. We say under ground, but not under earth; and we speak of the globe as divided into land and water, not into ground and water. Yet ground, earth and land are often used synonymously. We say, the produce or fruits of the ground, of the earth, or of land. The water overflows the low ground, or the low land. There was not a man to till the ground. Genesis 2:5. The ground shall give its increase. Zechariah 8:12. The fire ran along on the ground. Exodus 9:23.
Region; territory; as Egyptian ground; British ground; heavenly ground.
Land; estate; possession. Thy next design is on thy neighbor’s grounds.
The surface of the earth, or a floor or pavement. Dagon had fallen on his face to the ground. 7 Samuel 5:4.
Foundation; that which supports any thing. This argument stands on defensible ground. Hence,
groundage: A tax paid by a ship for standing in port.
ground-angling: Fishing without a float, with a bullet placed a few inches from the hook.
ground-ash: A sapling of ash; a young shoot from the stump of an ash.
ground-bait: Bait for fish which sinks to the bottom of the water.
ground-floor: The first or lower floor of a house. But the English call the second floor from the ground the first floor.
ground-ivy: A well known plant, the Glechoma hederacea; called also alehoof and gill.
groundless: Wanting ground or foundation; wanting cause or reason for support; as groundless fear.
Not authorized; false; as a groundless report or assertion.
groundlessly: Without reason or cause; without authority for support.
groundlessness: Want of just cause, reason or authority for support.
groundling: A fish that keeps at the bottom of the water; hence, a low vulgar person.
ground-nut: A plant, the Arachis, a native of South America.
ground-pine: A plant, a species of Teucrium or germander; said to be so called from its resinous smell.
ground-plate: In architecture, the ground-plates are the outermost pieces of timber lying on or near the ground, framed into one another with mortises and tenons.
ground-plot: The ground on which a building is placed.
ground-rent: Rent paid for the privilege of building on another man’s land.
ground-room: A room on the ground; a lower room.
groundsel: A plant of the genus Senecio, of several species.
The timber of a building which lies next to the ground; commonly called a sill.
ground-sill: The timber of a building which lies next to the ground; commonly called a sill.
ground-tackle: In ships, the ropes and furniture belonging to anchors.
groundwork: The work which forms the foundation or support of any thing; the basis; the fundamentals.
The ground; that to which the rest are additional.
group: A cluster, crowd or throng; an assemblage, either of persons or things; a number collected without any regular form or arrangement; as a group of men or of trees; a group of isles.
In painting and sculpture, an assemblage of two or more figures of men, beasts or other things which have some relation to each other.

To form a group; to bring or place together in a cluster or knot; to form an assemblage. The difficulty lies in drawing and disposing, or as the painters term it, in grouping such a multitude of different objects.
groop: A cluster, crowd or throng; an assemblage, either of persons or things; a number collected without any regular form or arrangement; as a group of men or of trees; a group of isles.
In painting and sculpture, an assemblage of two or more figures of men, beasts or other things which have some relation to each other.

To form a group; to bring or place together in a cluster or knot; to form an assemblage. The difficulty lies in drawing and disposing, or as the painters term it, in grouping such a multitude of different objects.
grouped: Formed or placed in a crowd.
grooped: Formed or placed in a crowd.
grouping: Bringing together in a cluster or assemblage.
The art of composing or combining the objects of a picture or piece of sculpture.
grooping: Bringing together in a cluster or assemblage.
grouse: A heath-cock or cock of the wood, a fowl of the genus Tetrao. The name is given to several species, forming a particular division of the genus; such as the black game, the red game, the ptarmigan, the ruffed grouse
grove: In gardening, a small wood or cluster of trees with a shaded avenue, or a wood impervious to the rays of the sun. A grove is either open or close; open, when consisting of large trees whose branches shade the ground below; close, when consisting of trees and underwood, which defend the avenues from the rays of the sun and from violent winds.
A wood of small extent. In America, the word is applied to a wood of natural growth in the field, as well as to planted trees in a garden, but only to a wood of small extent and not to a forest.
Something resembling a wood or trees in a wood. Tall groves of masts arose in beauteous pride.
grovel: To creep on the earth, or with the face to the ground; to lie prone, or move with the body prostrate on the earth; to act in a prostrate posture. Gaze on and grovel on thy face. To creep and grovel on the ground.
To be low or mean; a groveling sense; groveling thoughts.
groveler: One who grovels; an abject wretch.
grovy: Pertaining to a grove; frequenting groves.
grow: To enlarge in bulk or stature, by a natural, imperceptible addition of matter, through ducts and secreting organs, as animal and vegetable bodies; to vegetate as plants, or to be augmented by natural process, as animals. Thus, a plant grows from a seed to a shrub or tree, and a human being grows from a fetus to a man. He causeth the grass to grow for cattle. Psalm 104:14.
To be produced by vegetation; as, wheat grows in most parts of the world; rice grows only in warm climates.
To increase; to be augmented; to wax; as, a body grows larger by inflation or distension; intemperance is a growing evil.
To advance; to improve; to make progress; as, to grow in grace, in knowledge, in piety. The young man is growing in reputation.
grower: One who grows; that which increases.
In English use, one who raises or produces.
growing: Increasing; advancing in size or extent; becoming; accruing; swelling; thriving.
growl: To murmur or snarl, as a dog; to utter an angry, grumbling sound.
The murmur of a cross dog.
grown: of grow. Advanced; increased in growth.
Having arrived at full size or stature; as a grown woman. Grown over, covered by the growth of any thing; overgrown.
growth: The gradual increase of animal and vegetable bodies; the process of springing from a germ, seed or root, and proceeding to full size, by the addition of matter, through ducts and secretory vessels. In plantsWe speak of slow growth and rapid growth; of early growth; late growth and full growth.
Product; produce; that which has grown; as a fine growth of wood.
Production; any thing produced; as a poem of English growth.
Increase in number, bulk or frequency.
Increase in extent or prevalence; as the growth of trade; the growth of vice.
grub: A small worm; particularly, a hexapod or six-footed worm, produced from the egg of the beetle, which is transformed into a winged insect.
To dig; to be occupied in digging.
To dig; mostly followed by up. To grub up, is to dig up by the roots with an instrument; to root out by digging, or throwing out the soil; as, to grub up trees, rushes or sedge.
A short thick man; a dwarf, in contempt.
grubbing-hoe: An instrument for digging up trees, shrubsby the roots; a mattoc; called also a grub-ax.
grubble: To feel in the dark; to grovel.
grubstreet: Originally, the name of a street near Moorfields, in London, much inhabited by mean writers; hence applied to mean writings; as a Grubstreet poem.
grudge: To be discontented at another’s enjoyments or advantages; to envy one the possession or happiness which we desire for ourselves. ‘Tis not in thee . To grudge my pleasures, to cut off my train. I have often heard the presbyterians say, they did not grudge us our employments. It is followed by two objects, but probably by ellipsis; as, grudge us for grudge to us.
To give or take unwillingly. Nor grudge my cold embraces in the grave. They have grudged those contributions, which have set our country at the head of all the governments of Europe.
To murmur; to repine; to complain; as, to grudge or complain of injustice.
To be unwilling or reluctant. Not to serve your country.
To be envious. Grudge not one against another. James 5:9.
grudging: Envying; being uneasy at another’s possession of something which we have a desire to possess.
Uneasiness at the possession of something by another.
Reluctance; also, a secret wish or desire. He had a grudging still to be a knave.
grudgingly: Unwillingly; with reluctance or discontent; as, to give grudgingly.
gruel: A kind of light food made by boiling meal in water. It is usually made of the meal of oats or maiz.
gruff: Of a rough or stern countenance; sour; surly; severe; rugged; harsh.
gruffly: Roughly; sternly; ruggedly; harshly. --And gruffly looked the god.
grum: Morose; severe of countenance; sour; surly.
Low; deep in the throat; guttural; rumbling; as a grum voice.
grumble: To murmur with discontent; to utter a low voice by way of complaint. L’Avare, not using half his store, Still grumbles that he has no more.
To growl; to snarl; as a lion grumbling over his prey.
To rumble; to roar; to make a harsh and heavy sound; as grumbling thunder; a grumbling storm.
grumbler: One who grumbles or murmurs; one who complains; a discontented man.
grumbling: A murmuring through discontent; a rumbling.
grume: A thick viscid consistence of a fluid; a clot, as of blood
grumous: Thick; concreted; clotted; as grumous blood.
grumousness: A state of being clotted or concreted.
grunt: To murmur like a hog; to utter a short groan or a deep guttural sound.
A deep guttural sound, as a hog.
grunter: A fish of the gurnard king.
grunting: Uttering the murmuring or guttural sound of swine or other animals.
The guttural sound of swine and other animals.
grutch: for grudge, is now vulgar, and not to be used.
gry: A measure containing one tenth of a line.
Any thing very small or of little value.
gryphite: Crowstone, an oblong fossil shell, narrow at the head, and wider towards the extremity, where it ends in a circular limb; the head or beak is very hooked.
guaiacum: gua’cum. Lignum vitae, or pock wood; a tree produced in the warm climates of America. The wood is very hard, ponderous and resinous. The resin of this tree, or gum guaiacum, is of a greenish cast, and much used in medicine as a stimulant.
guana: A species of lizard, found in the warmer parts of America.
guanaco: The lama, or camel of South America, in a wild state.
guano: A substance found on many isles in the Pacific, which are frequented by fowls; used as a manure.
guara: A bird of Brazil, the Tantalus ruber, about the size of a spoonbill. When first hatched, it is black; it afterward changes to gray, and then to vivid red.
guarantor: gar’antor. A warrantor; one who engages to see that the stipulations of another are performed; also, one who engages to secure another in any right or possession.
guaranty: To warrant; to make sure; to undertake or engage that another person shall perform what he has stipulated; to oblige one’s self to see that another's engagements are performed; to secure the performance of; as, to guaranty the execution of a treaty.

To undertake to secure to another, at all events, as claims, rights or possessions. Thus in the treaty of 1778, France guarantied to the United States their liberty, sovereignty and independence, and their possessions; and the United States guarantied to France its possessions in America. The United States shall guaranty to every state in the Union a republican form of government.

An undertaking or engagement by a third person or party, that the stipulations of a treaty shall be observed by the contracting parties or by one of them; an undertaking that the engagement or promise of another shall be performed.
guard: To secure against injury, loss or attack; to protect; to defend; to keep in safety. We guard a city by walls and forts. A harbor is guarded by ships, booms or batteries. Innocence should be guarded by prudence and piety. Let observation and experience guard us against temptations to vice.
To secure against objections or the attacks of malevolence. Homer has guarded every circumstance with caution.
To accompany and protect; to accompany for protection; as, to guard a general on a journey; to guard the baggage of an army.
To adorn with lists, laces or ornaments.
guard-boat: A boat appointed to row the rounds among ships of war in a harbor, to observe that their officers keep a good look-out.
guard-room: A room for the accommodation of guards.
guard-ship: A vessel of war appointed to superintend the marine affairs in a harbor or river, and to receive impressed seamen.
guardant: In heraldry, having the face turned toward the spectator.
guarded: Defended; protected; accompanied by a guard; provided with means of defense.
Cautions; circumspect. He was guarded in his expressions.
Framed or uttered with caution; as, his expressions were guarded.
guardian: A warden; one who guards, preserves or secures; one to whom any thing is committed for preservation from injury.

In law, one who is chosen or appointed to take charge of the estate and education of an orphan who is a minor, or of any person who is not of sufficient discretion to manage his own concerns. The person committed to the care of a guardian is called his ward. Guardian of the spiritualities, the person to whom the spiritual jurisdiction of a diocese is entrusted, during the vacancy of the see.
Protection; performing the office of a protector; as a guardian angel; guardian care.
guardianship: The office of a guardian; protection; care; watch.
guarding: Defending; protecting; securing; attending for protection.
guava: An American tree, and its fruit, of the genus Psidium. It is of two species, or rather varieties, the pyriferum or white guava, and pomiferum or red guava. The fruit or berry is large and oval-shaped, like a pomegranate, which it resembles in its astringent quality. The pulp is of an agreeable flavor, and of this fruit is made a delicious jelly.
gubernatorial: Pertaining to government, or to a governor.
gudgeon: gud’jin. A small fish of the genus Cyprinus, a fish easily caught, and hence,
A person easily cheated or ensnared.
A bait; allurement; something to be caught to a man’s disadvantage.
An iron pin on which a wheel turns. Sea-gudgeon, the black goby or rock fish.
guelf: The Guelfs, so called from the name of a family, composed a faction formerly in Italy, opposed to the Gibelines.
guelph: The Guelfs, so called from the name of a family, composed a faction formerly in Italy, opposed to the Gibelines.
guerdon: ger’don. A reward; requital; recompense; in a good or bad sense.
guess: To conjecture; to form an opinion without certain principles or means of knowledge; to judge at random, either of a present unknown fact, or of a future fact. First, if thou canst, the harder reason guess.
To judge or form an opinion from some reasons that render a thing probable, but fall short of sufficient evidence. From slight circumstances or occasional expressions, we guess an author’s meaning.
To conjecture; to judge at random. We do not know which road to take, but we must guess at it.
guesser: One who guesses; a conjecturer; one who judges or gives an opinion without certain means of knowing.
guessing: Conjecturing; judging without certain evidence, or grounds of opinion.
guest: A stranger; one who comes from a distance, and takes lodgings at a place, either for a night or for a longer time.
A visitor; a stranger or friend, entertained in the house or at the table of another, whether by invitation or otherwise. The wedding was furnished with guests. Vatihew 22:10.
guest-chamber: An apartment appropriated to the entertainment of guests. Mark 14:14.
guest-rope: A rope to tow with, or to make fast a boat.
guess-rope: A rope to tow with, or to make fast a boat.
guestwise: In the manner of a guest.
guhr: A loose, earthy deposit from water, found in the cavities or clefts of rocks, mostly white, but sometimes red or yellow, from a mixture of clay or ocher.
guidable: That may be guided or governed by counsel.
guidage: The reward given to a guide for services.
guidance: The act of guiding; direction; government; a leading. Submit to the guidance of age and wisdom.
guide: To lead or direct in a way; to conduct in a course or path; as, to guide an enemy or a traveler, who is not acquainted with the road or course. The meek will he guide in judgment. Psalm 25:9.
To direct; to order. He will guide his affairs with discretion. Psalm 112:5.
To influence; to give direction to. Men are guided by their interest, or supposed interest.
To instruct and direct. Let parents guide their children to virtue, dignity and happiness.
guided: Led; conducted; directed in the way; instructed and directed.
guideless: Destitute of a guide; wanting a director.
guidepost: A post at the forks of a road, for directing travelers the way.
guider: A guide; one who guides or directs.
guidon: The flag or standard of a troop of cavalry; or the standard-bearer.
guild: In England, a society, fraternity or company, associated for some purpose, particularly for carrying on commerce. The merchant-guilds of our ancestors, answer to our modern corporations. They were licensed by the king, and governed by laws and orders of their own. Hence the name Guild-hall, the great court of judicature in London.
guile: Craft; cunning; artifice; duplicity; deceit; usually in a bad sense. We may, with more successful hope, resolve . To wage by force or guile eternal war. Behold an Israelite indeed, in whom is no guile. John 1:47.
guileful: Cunning; drafty; artful; wily; deceitful; insidious; as a guileful person.
Intended to deceive; as guileful words.
guildless: Free from guile or deceit; artless; frank; sincere; honest.
guiler: One who betrays into danger by insidious arts.
guillemot: A water fowl of the genus Colymbus, and order of ansers. It is found in the northern parts of Europe, Asia, and America.
guillotin: An engine or machine for beheading persons at a stroke.
guilt: Criminality; that state of a moral agent which results from his actual commission of a crime or offense, knowing it to be a crime, or violation of law. To constitute guilt there must be a moral agent enjoying freedom of will, and capable of distinguishing between right and wrong, and a wilful or intentional violation of a known law, or rule of duty. The guilt of a person exists, as soon as the crime is committed; but to evince it to others, it must be proved by confession, or conviction in due course of law. Guilt renders a person a debtor to the law, as it binds him to pay a penalty in money or suffering. Guilt therefore implies both criminality and liableness to punishment. Guilt may proceed either from a positive act or breach of law, or from voluntary neglect of known duty.
Criminality in a political or civil view; exposure to forfeiture or other penalty. A ship incurs guilt by the violation of a blockade.
guiltily: In a manner to incur guilt, not innocently.
guiltiness: The state of being guilty; wickedness; criminality; guilt.
guiltless: Free from guilt, crime or offense; innocent. The Lord will not hold him guiltless, that taketh his name in vain. Exodus 20:7.
Not produced by the slaughter of animals. But from the mountain’s grassy side\ . A guiltless feast I bring.
guiltlessness: Innocence; freedom from guilt or crime.
guilty: gilt’y. Criminal; having knowingly committed a crime or offense, or having violated a law by an overt act or by neglect, and by that act or neglect, being liable to punishment; not innocent. It may be followed by of; as, to be guilty of theft or arson. Nor he, nor you, were guilty of the strife.
Wicked; corrupt; sinful; as a guilty world.

Conscious. In Scripture, to be guilty of death, is to have committed a crime which deserves death. Matthew 26:66. To be guilty of the body and blood of Christ, is to be chargeable with the crime of crucifying Christ afresh, and offering indignity to his person and righteousness, represented by the symbols of the Lord’s supper. 7 Corinthians 11:27.
guinea: gin’ny. Formerly, a gold coin in Great Britain of the value of twenty one shillings sterling, equal to $4.66 2/3rds, American money.
guinea-dropper: One who cheats by dropping guineas.
guinea-hen: The Numida meleagris, a fowl of the gallinaceous order, a native of Africa. It is larger than the common domestic hen, and has a kind of colored fleshy horn on each side of the head. Its color is a dark gray, beautifully variegated with small white spots.
guinea-pepper: A plant, the Capsicum. The pods of some species are used for pickles.
guinea-pig: In zoology, a quadruped of the genus Cavia or cavy, found in Brazil. It is about seven inches in length, and of a white color, variegated with spots of orange and black.
guiniad: The whiting, a fish of the salmon or trout kind, found in many lakes in Europe and in Hudson’s bay. It is gregarious, and may be taken in vast numbers at a draught.
gwiniad: The whiting, a fish of the salmon or trout kind, found in many lakes in Europe and in Hudson’s bay. It is gregarious, and may be taken in vast numbers at a draught.
guise: External appearance; dress; garb. He appeared in the guise of a shepherd. The hypocrite wears the guise of religion. That love which is without dissimulation, wears not the guise of modern liberality.
Manner; mien; cast of behavior. By their guise . Just men they seem.
Custom; mode; practice. The swain replied, it never was our guise, To slight the poor, or aught humane despise.
guiser: gi’zer. A person in disguise; a mummer who goes about at Christmas.
guitar: git‘ar. A stringed instrument of music; in England and the United States, used chiefly by ladies, but in Spain and Italy, much used by men.
gula: An ogee or wavy member in a building; the cymatium.
gulaund: An aquatic fowl of a size between a duck and a goose; the breast and belly white; the head mallard green. It inhabits Iceland.
gulch: A glutton; a swallowing or devouring.
gules: In heraldry, a term denoting red, intended perhaps to represent courage, animation or hardihood.
gulf: A recess in the ocean from the general line of the shore into the land, or a tract of water extending from the ocean or a sea into the land, between two points or promontories; a large bay; as the gulf of Mexico; the gulf of Venice; the gulf of Finland. A gulf and a bay differ only in extent. We apply bay to a large or small recess of the sea, as the bay of Biscay, the bay of Fundy; but gulf is applied only to a large extent of water.
An abyss; a deep place in the earth; as the gulf of Avernus.
gulfy: Full of whirlpools or gulfs; as a gulfy sea.
gull: To deceive; to cheat; to mislead by deception; to trick; to defraud. The vulgar, gull’d into rebellion.
A cheating or cheat; trick; fraud.
A marine fowl of the genus Larus, and order of ansers. There are several species.
gullcatcher: A cheat; a man who cheats or entraps silly people.
gullet: The passage in the neck of an animal by which food and liquor are taken into the stomach; the esophagus.
gullied: Having a hollow worn by water.
gully: A channel or hollow worn in the earth by a current of water.
To wear a hollow channel in the earth.
gullyhole: An opening where gutters empty their contents into the subterraneous sewer.
gulosity: Greediness; voracity; excessive appetite for food.
gulp: To swallow eagerly, or in large draughts. To gulp up, to throw up from the throat or stomach; to disgorge.
A swallow, or as much as is swallowed at once.
gum: The hard fleshy substance of the jaws which invests the teeth.

The mucilage of vegetables; a concrete juice which exudes through the bark of trees, and thickens on the surface. It is soluble in water, to which it gives a viscous and adhesive quality. It is insoluble in alcohol, and coagulates in weak acids. When dry, it is transparent and brittle, not easily pulverized, and of an insipid or slightly saccharine taste. Gum differs from resin in several particulars, but custom has inaccurately given the name of gum to several resins and gum-resins, as gum-copal. gum-sandarach, gum-ammoniac, and others. The true gums are gumarabic, gum-senegal, gum-tragacanth, and the gums of the peach, plum and cherry treesGum-elastic, or Elastic-gum, is a singular substance, obtained from a tree in America by incision. It is a white juice, which, when dry, becomes very tough and elastic, and is used for bottles, surgical instruments.
To unite by a viscous substance.
gum-arabic: A gum which flows from the acacia, in Arabia, Egypt
gumlac: The produce of an insect which deposits its eggs on the branches of a tree called bihar, in Assam, a country bordering on Tibet, and elsewhere in Asia.
gum-resin: A mixed juice of plants, consisting of resin and an extractive matter, which has been taken for a gummy substance. The gum-resins do not flow naturally from plants, but are mostly extracted by incision, in the form of white, yellow or red emulsive fluids, which dry and consolidate. The most important species are olibanum, galbanum, scammony, gamboge, euphorbium, assafetida, aloes, myrrh, and gum-ammoniac. Gum-resins are natural combinations of gum and resin. Gum-resins are composed of a gum or extractive matter, and a body intermediate between oil and resin; to which last they owe their peculiar properties.
gum-senegal: A gum resembling gum-arabic, brought from the country of the river Senegal in Africa.
gum-tragacanth: The gum of a thorny shrub of that name, in Crete, Asia and Greece.
gumminess: The state or quality of being gummy; viscousness.
gummosity: The nature of gum; gumminess; a viscous or adhesive quality.
gummous: Of the nature or quality of gum; viscous; adhesive.
gummy: Consisting of gum; of the nature of gum; viscous; adhesive.
Covered with gum or viscous matter.
gun: An instrument consisting of a barrel or tube of iron or other metal fixed in a stock, from which balls, shot or other deadly weapons are discharged by the explosion of gunpowder. The larger species of guns are called cannon; and the smaller species are called muskets, carbines, fowling piecesBut one species of fire-arms, the pistol, is never called a gun.
gun-barrel: The barrel or tube of a gun.
gunboat: A boat or small vessel fitted to carry a gun or two at the bow.
gun-carriage: A wheel carriage for bearing and moving cannon.
gunnel: The upper edge of a ship’s side; the uppermost wale of a ship, or that piece of timber which reaches on either side from the quarter-deck to the fore- castle, being the uppermost bend which finishes the upper works of the hull.
gunner: One skilled in the use of guns; a cannoneer; an officer appointed to manage artillery. The gunner of a ship of war has the charge of the ammunition and artillery, and his duty is to keep the latter in good order, and to teach the men the exercise of the guns.
gunnery: The act of charging, directing and firing guns, as cannon, mortars and the like. Gunnery is founded on the science of projectiles.
gunning: The act of hunting or shooting game with a gun.
gunpowder: A composition of saltpeter, sulphur and charcoal, mixed and reduced to a fine powder, then granulated and dried. It is used in artillery, in shooting game, in blasting rocks
gunroom: In ships, an apartment on the after end of the lower gun-deck, occupied by the gunner, or by the lieutenants as a mess-room.
gunshot: The distance of the point blank range of a cannon-shot.
Made by the shot of a gun; as a gunshot wound.
gunsmith: A maker of small arms; one whose occupation is to make or repair small fire-arms.
gunsmithery: The business of a gunsmith; the art of making small firearms.
gunstick: A rammer, or ramrod; a stick or rod to ram down the charge of a musket
gunstock: The stock or wood in which the barrel of a gun is fixed.
gunstone: A stone used for the shot of cannon. Before the invention of iron balls, stones were used for shot.
guntackle: The tackle used on board of ships to run the guns out of the ports, and to secure them at sea. The tackles are pulleys affixed to the sides of a gun-carriage.
gunwale: The upper edge of a ship’s side; the uppermost wale of a ship, or that piece of timber which reaches on either side from the quarter-deck to the fore- castle, being the uppermost bend which finishes the upper works of the hull.
gurgion: The coarser part of meal separated from the bran.
gurgle: To run as liquor with a purling noise; to run or flow in a broken, irregular, noisy current, as water from a bottle, or a small stream on a stony bottom. Pure gurgling rills the lonely desert trace.
gurgling: Running or flowing with a purling sound.
gurhofite: A subvariety of magnesian carbonate of lime, found near Gurhof, in Lower Austria. It is snow white, and has a dull, slightly conchoidal, or even fracture.
gurnard: A fish of several species of the genus Trigla. The head is loricated with rough lines, or bony plates, and there are seven rays in the membranes of the gills.
gurrah: A kind of plain, coarse India muslin.
gush: To issue with violence and rapidity, as a fluid; to rush forth as a fluid from confinement; as, blood gushes from a vein in venesection. Behold, he smote the rock, that the waters gushed out. Psalm 78:20.
To flow copiously. Tears gushed from her eyes.
To emit in copious effusion. The gaping wound gushed out a crimson flood.
A sudden and violent issue of a fluid from an inclosed place; an emission of liquor in a large quantity and with force; the fluid thus emitted.
gushing: Rushing forth with violence, as a fluid; flowing copiously; as gushing waters.
gusset: A small piece of cloth inserted in a garment, for the purpose of strengthening or enlarging some part.
gust: Taste; tasting, or the sense of tasting. More generally, the pleasure of tasting; relish.
Sensual enjoyment. Where love is duty on the female side, On theirs, mere sensual gust, and sought with surly pride.
Pleasure; amusement; gratification. Destroy all creatures for thy sport or gust.
Turn of fancy; intellectual taste. A choice of it may be made according to the gust and manner of the ancients.
To taste; to have a relish.
gusto: Relish; that which excites pleasant sensations in the palate or tongue.
gusty: Subject to sudden blasts of wind; stormy; tempestuous. Once upon a raw and gusty day, The troubled Tyber chafing with his shores-- .
gut: The intestinal canal of an animal; a pipe or tube extending, with many circumvolutions, from the pylorus to the vent. This pipe is composed of three coats, and is attached to the body by a membrane called the mesentery. This canal is of different sizes in different parts, and takes different names. The thin and small parts are called the duodenum, the ilium, and the jejunum; the large and thick parts are called the eaecum, the colon, the rectum. By this pipe, the undigested and unabsorbed parts of food are conveyed from the stomach and discharged. This word in the plural is applied to the whole mass formed by its natural convolutions in the abdomen.
The stomach; the receptacle of food.
To take out the bowels; to eviscerate.
gutta serena: In medicine, amaurosis; blindness occasioned by a diseased retina.
gutted: Deprived of the bowels; eviscerated; deprived of contents.
gutter: A channel for water; a hollow piece of timber, or a pipe, for catching and conveying off the water which drops from the eaves of a building.
A channel or passage for water; a hollow in the earth for conveying water; and, in popular usage, a channel worn in the earth by a current of water.
To cut or form into small hollows.
To run or sweat as a candle.
guttulous: In the form of a small drop, or of small drops.
guttural: Pertaining to the throat; formed in the throat; as a guttural letter or sound; a guttural voice.
A letter pronounced in the throat; as the Gr. x.
gutturally: In a guttural manner; in the throat.
gutty: In heraldry, charged or sprinkled with drops.
guy: In marine affairs, a rope used to keep a heavy body steady while hoisting or lowering; also, a tackle to confine a boom forwards, when a vessel is going large, and to prevent the sail from gybing. Guy is also a large slack rope, extending from the head of the main-mast to that of the fore-mast, to sustain a tackle for loading or unloading.
guzzle: To swallow liquor greedily; to drink much; to drink frequently. Well seasoned bowls the gossip’s spirits raise, Who, while she guzzles, chats the Doctor’s praise .
To swallow much or often; to swallow with immoderate gust. --Still guzzling must of wine.
guzzler: One who guzzles; an immoderate drinker.
gybe: In seamen’s language, to shift a boom-sail from one side of a vessel to the other.
gybing: Shifting a boom-sail from one side of a vessel to the other.
gye: To guide. H, is the eighth letter of the English Alphabet. It is properly the representative of the Chaldee, Syriac and Hebrew, which is the eighth letter in those alphabets. Its form is the same as the Greek H eta. It is not strictly a vowel, nor an articulation; but the mark of a stronger breathing, than that which precedes the utterance of any other letter. It is pronounced with an expiration of breath, which, preceding a vowel, is perceptible by the ear at a considerable distance. Thus, harm and arm, hear and ear, heat and eat, are distinguished at almost any distance at which the voice can be heard. H is a letter sui generis, but as useful in forming and distinguishing words as any other.
gadefly: An insect of the genus Oestrus, which stings cattle, and deposits its eggs in their skin; called also the breeze.
gallefly: The insect that punctures plants and occasions galls; the cynips.
generalshipp: The skill and conduct of a general officer; military skill in a commander, exhibited in the judicious arrangements of troops, or the operations of war.
ginghanm: A kind or striped cotton cloth.
gymnasium: In Greece, a place where athletic exercises were performed. Hence, a place of exercise; a school.
gymnastic: Pertaining to athletic exercises of the body, intended for health, defense or diversion, as running, leaping, wrestling, throwing the discus, the javelin or the hoop, playing with ballsThe modern gymnastic exercises are intended chiefly for the preservation and promotion of health.
gymnastics: The gymnastic art; the art of performing athletic exercises.
gymnic: Pertaining to athletic exercises of the body.
gymnosophist: A philosopher of India, so called from his going with bare feet, or with little clothing. The Gymnosophists in India lived in the woods and on mountains, subsisting on wild productions of the earth. They never drank wine nor married. Some of them traveled about, and practiced physic. They believed the immortality and transmigration of the soul. They placed the chief happiness of man in a contempt of the goods of fortune, and of the pleasures of sense.
gymnosperm: In botany, a plant that bears naked seeds.
gymnospermous: Having naked seeds, or seeds not inclosed in a capsule or other vessel.
gynander: In botany, a plant whose stamens are inserted in the pistil.
gynandrian: Having stamens inserted in the pistil.
gypseous: Of the nature of gypsum; partaking of the qualities of gypsum.
gypsum: Plaster stone; sulphate of lime; a mineral not infrequently found in crystals, often in amorphous masses. There are several subspecies and varieties; as the foliated, compact, earthy, granular, snowy and branchy. Gypsum is of great use in agriculture and the arts. As a manure, it is invaluable.
gyral: Whirling; moving in a circular form.
gyration: A turning or whirling round; a circular motion.
gyre: A circular motion, or a circle described by a moving body; a turn. Quick and more quick he spins in giddy gyres.
gyrfalcon: A species of Falco, or hawk.
gyromancy: A kind of divination performed by walking round in a circle or ring.
gyve: Gyves are fetters or shackles for the legs. Gyves and the mill had tamed thee.

To fetter; to shackle; to chain. H H, is the eighth letter of the English Alphabet. It is properly the representative of the Chaldee, Syriac and Hebrew, which is the eighth letter in those alphabets. Its form is the same as the Greek H eta. It is not strictly a vowel, nor an articulation; but the mark of a stronger breathing, than that which precedes the utterance of any other letter. It is pronounced with an expiration of breath, which, preceding a vowel, is perceptible by the ear at a considerable distance. Thus, harm and arm, hear and ear, heat and eat, are distinguished at almost any distance at which the voice can be heard. H is a letter sui generis, but as useful in forming and distinguishing words as any other. In our mother tongue, the Anglo-Saxon, and other Teutonic dialects, h sometimes represents the L. c, and the Gr. x; as in horn, L. cornu, Gr. to hide; G. haut, Sw. hud, D.
